instruction: What is Seccomp and how does it relate to Kubernetes?
response: Seccomp stands for secure computing mode and has been a feature of the Linux kernel since version 2.6.12, allowing processes to sandbox their privileges by restricting calls from userspace into the kernel. In Kubernetes, seccomp profiles can be automatically applied to Pods and containers.
title: Seccomp and Kubernetes

instruction: How many ways are there to specify a Seccomp profile for a pod in Kubernetes?
response: There are four methods of specifying a seccomp profile for a pod: using the `spec.securityContext.seccompProfile` field, setting it on individual containers within a Pod via their respective security contexts, doing so with an (restartable / sidecar) init container's security context, and applying to ephemeral containers by specifying in their security context.
title: Seccomp and Kubernetes

instruction: What happens if the seccompProfile field is not set explicitly for pod components like ephemeral containers or init containers?
response: If a component such as an ephemeral container does not specify its own `seccompProfile` and it inherits from another level (like Pod), then by default, it will inherit the seccomp profile of that higher-level resource. If no explicit value is set for pods or containers themselves, they would use inherited values unless specified otherwise.
title: Seccomp and Kubernetes

instruction: What is the Container Runtime Interface (CRI) in Kubernetes?
response: The CRI is a plugin interface which enables the kubelet to use various container runtimes without recompiling cluster components.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI)

instruction: Which command line flag can be used within the kubelet for configuring runtime and image service endpoints?
response: --container-runtime-endpoint
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI)

instruction: What happens if a container runtime does not support the v1 CRI API when upgrading Kubernetes to version 1.26 or later on a node?
response: The kubelet will fail to register and report an error.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI)

instruction: How can I adapt my Kubernetes cluster to the needs of a distributed microservices architecture?
response: Use Custom Resources and set up an Extension API Server.
title: Extend Kubernetes

instruction: What is recommended for accessing the Kubernetes API from within corporate firewalls that block standard ports like HTTP/HTTPS?
response: Configure multiple schedulers, use a SOCKS5 proxy to access the Kubernetes API.
title: Extend Kubernetes

instruction: How can I monitor and manage my extended Kubernetes cluster effectively after customization for specific needs?
response: Feedback is encouraged on Stack Overflow or by opening an issue in the GitHub Repository, including index files as needed based on modifications made to extend Kubernetes.
title: Extend Kubernetes

instruction: What is the purpose of ParamRef in Kubernetes?
response: ParamRef describes how to locate parameters for use as input to expressions applied by a policy binding.
title: ParamRef

instruction: When using name and selector properties, what must be true about their usage according to the documentation on 'name' field?
response: One of `name` or `selector` must be set, but they are mutually exclusive. If one is set, the other must be unset.
title: ParamRef

instruction: How can you attach kubectl to get output from running pod mypod?
response: kubectl attach mypod
title: kubectl attach

instruction: What command would allow attaching stdin and tty to a container named 'ruby-container' in the same pod, while also providing raw terminal mode output back to the client?
response: kubectl attach mypod -c ruby-container -i -t
title: kubectl attach

instruction: If you want kubectl to wait for a certain amount of time until at least one pod is running before attempting an attachment, which flag would be used and what does it default to?
response:
-q,--quiet
--pod-running-timeout duration
default: 1m0s
title: kubectl attach

instruction: What is the purpose of creating a Kubernetes Deployment object?
response: To run an application.
title: Run a Stateless Application Using a Deployment

instruction: How can you display information about the created Nginx deployment in your cluster using kubectl command-line tool?
response: Use 'kubectl describe deployment nginx-deployment'
title: Run a Stateless Application Using a Deployment

instruction: What is indicated by a Deployment strategy type of RollingUpdate with max unavailable set to 1 and max surge also at 1 in the context of this Nginx application?
response: The update will only affect one pod at a0 time, allowing new replicas to be created while maintaining service availability.
title: Run a Stateless Application Using a Deployment

instruction: What is the purpose of Resource Quotas in Kubernetes?
response: Resource quotas are used to limit aggregate resource consumption per namespace, ensnerving fair usage among multiple users or teams sharing a cluster.
title: Resource Quotas

instruction: How does Kubernetes enforce quota constraints when creating resources like pods in a namespace?
response: The control plane tracks the creation of objects and their resource requests/limits. If these violate any hard-set quota, it rejects the request with an HTTP 403 Forbidden status code.
title: Resource Quotas

instruction: What happens if a user tries to create resources without specifying compute resource requisites in Kubernetes when quotas are enabled?
response: The quota system may reject pod creation, as users must specify requests or limits for those values according to the set quotas.
title: Resource Quotas

instruction: How do you view the rollout history of a deployment in Kubernetes?
response: You use the command `kubectl rollout history deployment/abc`.
title: kubectl rollout history

instruction: What flag would allow ignoring errors when missing fields or map keys are encountered during template processing, and how does it apply to output formats?
response: '--allow-missing-template-keys' allows this behavior. It applies only to golang and jsonpath output formats.
title: kubectl rollout history

instruction: How can you specify a revision when viewing the details of a DaemonSet in Kubernetes using kubectl rollout history?
response: '--revision=3' specifies that we want to see the details for revision number 3 of the specified DaemonSet.
title: kubectl rollout history

instruction: What is the purpose of an IPAddress in Kubernetes?
response: IPAddress represents a single IP of a single IP Family and is used by APIs that operate on IP addresses, specifically for allocation within the Service core API.
title: IPAddress

instruction: How are valid IPv4 and IPv6 formats represented in Kubernetes' documentation?
response: Valid IPv4 format suppresses leading zeros: 192.168.1.5, while the RFC 5952 representation is used for IPv6 without any suppression of leading zeroes.
title: IPAddress

instruction: What are some examples of valid and invalid representations given in Kubernetes documentation?
response: Valid: 192.168.1.5, 2001:db8::1 or 2001:db8:aaaa:bbbb:cccc:dddd:eeee:1; Invalid examples include suppressed leading zeros in IPv4 like 10.01.2.3 and an incomplete RFC 5952 representation for IPv6 as 2001:db8:0:0:0::1.
title: IPAddress

instruction: What does ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding bind in Kubernetes?
response: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding binds the ValidatingAdmissionPolicy with paramerized resources.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding

instruction: How many times will a policy be evaluated for each admission request if it uses parameters, according to the documentation?
response: Each binding causes its policy to be evaluated N times where N is equal to the number of parameters selected by the binding.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding

instruction: Can adding or removing policies affect whether an (policy, binding, param) combination stays within a given CEL budget for evaluations in Kubernetes?
response: No, changes like adding/removing policies, bindings, or params cannot affect if a specific (policy, binding, param) combo is within its own independent evaluation CEL cost budget.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding

instruction: How does Kubernetes facilitate both declarative configuration and automation for containerized workloads and services?
response: Kubernetes provides a portable, extensible open source platform that helps manage these aspects.
title: Concepts

instruction: How can one enable the collection of Pod and Container metrics via the CRI in Kubernetes?
response: Enable the 'PodAndContainerStatsFromCRI' feature gate, use a compatible CRI implementation (containerd >= 1.6.0 or CRI-O >= 1.23.0), and configure kubelet to poll for pod and container stats from the underlying runtime.
title: CRI Pod & Container Metrics

instruction: What are some benefits of using a CRI based collection mechanism instead of direct cAdvisor inspection?
response: Potential improved performance due to reusing data collected during normal operations by the container runtime and decoupling kubelet from processes directly on host, allowing metrics for runtimes that use virtualization.
title: CRI Pod & Container Metrics

instruction: What is required in terms of CRI implementation compatibility?
response: A compatible CRI implementation such as containerd >= 1.6.0 or CRI-O >= 1.23.0 must be used to utilize the CRI based collection mechanism.
title: CRI Pod & Container Metrics

instruction: How can you customize the behavior of kube-scheduler in Kubernetes?
response: By writing a configuration file and passing its path as a command line argument.
title: Scheduler Configuration

instruction: What is a scheduling Profile in the context of KubeSchedulerConfiguration, and how does it function?
response: A scheduling Profile allows you to configure different stages of scheduling within kube-scheduler. Each stage corresponds to an extension point that plugins implement.
title: Scheduler Configuration

instruction: What is the purpose of queueSort plugin in Kubernetes and when can it be enabled?
response: The queueSort plugin provides a function for sorting pending Pods within the scheduling queue. It should only be enabled at one time, as exactly one such plugin may run.
title: Scheduler Configuration

instruction: What is a lease in distributed systems and how does Kubernetes utilize it?
response: In distributed systems, a lease provides a mechanism to lock shared resources and coordinate activity between members of a set. In Kubernetes, Lease objects are used for system-critical capabilities such as node heartbeats and component-level leader election.
title: Leases

instruction: How does the control plane in Kubernetes use leases for leader election?
response: Kubernetes uses Leases to ensure only one instance of a component is running at any given time. This mechanism, based on updating the 'spec.renewTime' field within a Node Lease object with every kubelet heartbeat, helps in determining node availability and facilitates leader election among control plane components like kube-controller-manager.
title: Leases

instruction: What is the purpose of 'ControllerManagerReleaseLeaderElectionLockOnExit' feature gate in Kubernetes v1.36?
response: 'ControllerManagerReleaseLeaderElectionLockOnExit' allows the kube-controller-manager to actively release its leader election lock during leader transitions, which enables a new leader to be elected more quickly and reduces leadership transition latency.
title: Leases

instruction: How does Kubernetes expose information about itself to containers in a pod?
response: By using environment variables or Volume files, as explained by the downward API.
title: Expose Pod Information to Containers Through Files

instruction: What is required before beginning this task of exposing Pod fields through volume mounts?
response: A configured kubectl command-line tool to communicate with a Kubernetes cluster and at least two nodes that are not control plane hosts in the same zone.
title: Expose Pod Information to Containers Through Files

instruction: What is the purpose of creating a VolumeSnapshotClass in Kubernetes?
response: To describe the 'classes' of storage when provisioning a volume snapshot.
title: Volume Snapshot Classes

instruction: Which fields are contained within each VolumeSnapshotClass object and what do they signify?
response: The driver, deletionPolicy, and parameters. The driver specifies how the CSI is used to create snapsh0pshot volumes; deletionPolicy defines whether a snapshot should be deleted when its volume does as well (Delete); parameters allow administrators to specify default values for certain properties.
title: Volume Snapshot Classes

instruction: What happens if multiple VolumeSnapshotClasses exist with matching CSI drivers and no specific class is requested?
response: Kubernetes automatically selects a default VolumeSnapshotClass that has the appropriate driver match. If administrators want to specify different classes for each of their available CSI drivers, they can do so by setting up separate volume snapshot classes.
title: Volume Snapshot Classes

instruction: What resource types does WebhookAdmission Configuration (v1) provide configuration for?
response: WebhookAdmission provides configuration for webhook admission controller.
title: WebhookAdmission Configuration (v1)

instruction: Which file extensions are supported by the StaticManifestsDir field in the WebhookAdmissionConfiguration specification when loading static configurations at startup?
response: .yaml, .yml, and .json
title: WebhookAdmission Configuration (v1)

instruction: What is required to use the KubeConfigFile field within the WebhookAdmission Configuration (v1) for admission control in a namespace?
response: The ManifestBasedAdmissionControlConfig feature gate needs to be enabled.
title: WebhookAdmission Configuration (v1)

instruction: How do you display resource (CPU/memory) usage of pods using kubectl top pod?
response: The 'top pod' command allows you to see the CPU and memory consumption of pods.
title: kubectl top pod

instruction: What are some examples of how to use the kubectl top pod command with different options?
response: 'kubectl top pod --namespace=NAMESPACE', 'kubectl top pod POD_NAME --containers' and 'kubectl top pod -l name=myLabel'
title: kubectl top pod

instruction: What does the option --all-namespaces do when used with kubectl top pod?
response: '--all-namespaces' lists requested objects across all namespaces, ignoring the namespace in current context.
title: kubectl top pod

instruction: What are the two types of scheduling policies supported by Kubernetes for a PodGroup?
response: The two policy types supported by Kubernetes for a PodGroup are 'basic' and 'gang'.
title: PodGroup Scheduling Policies

instruction: How does the gang scheduling policy function in terms of pod placement within a group?
response: The gang scheduling policy enforces an "all-or-nothing" approach, requiring that all Pods must be scheduled simultaneously for any workload to make progress.
title: PodGroup Scheduling Policies

instruction: What additional field is required when using the 'gang' policy in a Kubernetes PodGroup and what does it signify?
response: 'minCount': This field specifies the minimum number of Pods that must be schedulable simultaneously for the group to be considered feasible, with its default value set at 4.
title: PodGroup Scheduling Policies

instruction: How can you restrict the number of Pods in a specific namespace?
response: By setting up a ResourceQuota object.
title: Configure a Pod Quota for a Namespace

instruction: What command would create a new namespace specifically for this exercise, named 'quota-pod-example'?
response: kubectl create namespace quota-pod-example
title: Configure a Pod Quota for a Namespace

instruction: How can you view detailed information about the ResourceQuota created in your specific namespace?
response: By using kubectl get resourcequota pod-demo --namespace=quota-pod-example --output yaml
title: Configure a Pod Quota for a Namespace

instruction: What is a CronJob in Kubernetes?
response: A CronJob object runs automated tasks at specified times.
title: Running Automated Tasks with a CronJob

instruction: How can you create and run the example 'hello' CronJob using kubectl command-line tool?
response: Create with `kubectl create -f https://k8s.io/examples/application/job/cronjob.yaml` and start watching jobs.
title: Running Automated Tasks with a CronJob

instruction: What does the output 'SUSPEND: False, ACTIVE: 0' indicate about a CronJob status?
response: 'ACTIVE': The number of currently running jobs; if it is zero, no job is currently executing.
title: Running Automated Tasks with a CronJob

instruction: What does the PolicyRule object in Kubernetes describe?
response: PolicyRule holds information that describes a policy rule, but it doesn't contain who or which namespace the rule applies to.
title: PolicyRule

instruction: In the context of PolicyRule, what is specified by 'apiGroups' and how does it function?
response: 'APIGroups' specifies the name(s) of an APIGroup that contains resources. If multiple groups are listed, any action requested against enumerated resources in those groups will be allowed.
title: PolicyRule

instruction: What is indicated by 'nonResourceURLs' within a PolicyRule and what does it imply?
response: 'NonResourceURLs' specifies partial URLs that access should have. The presence of an empty string at the end indicates allowed paths, but only as the final step in any path.
title: PolicyRule

instruction: What information does 'kubectl version' print?
response: The client and server version information for the current context.
title: kubectl version

instruction: What does setting '--match-server-version' to true do when using kubectl version command?
response: Require that the server version matches the client version.
title: kubectl version

instruction: How does CSIStorageCapacity help in the allocation of PersistentVolumes?
response: CSIStorageCapacity stores available capacity for a given StorageClass and topology segment, assisting in deciding where to instantiate new PVs.
title: CSIStorageCapacity

instruction: What happens when no suitable object exists with the right combination of storage class name and topology?
response: It implies that there is no capacity available for a certain combination.
title: CSIStorageCapacity

instruction: How does the kube-scheduler use CSIStorageCapacity in its scheduling process when CSIDriverSpec.StorageCapacity opts into it?
response: The scheduler compares MaximumVolumeSize against requested size and uses available capacity as filtered by StorageCapacity to allocate PVs.
title: CSIStorageCapacity

instruction: What is the main objective of performing a Rolling Update in Kubernetes?
response: To update an application with zero downtime by incrementally replacing current Pods with new ones.
title: Performing a Rolling Update

instruction: How does rolling updates ensure that users continue to access the updated application during deployment changes?
response: By routing traffic only to Pods serving requests, ensuring availability even while updating.
title: Performing a Rolling Update

instruction: What options can be configured for maximum unavailable and new created Pods during a Rolling Update in Kubernetes?
response: Both are configurable either as numbers or percentages of the total number of Pods. Default is one each, but this can change.
title: Performing a Rolling Update

instruction: What is an Admission Controller and what are its types?
response: An admission controller in Kubernetes intercepts requests to the API server before object persistence. It can be 'validating', which may reject a request, or 'mutating', which allows modification of objects it admits.
title: Glossary

instruction: What is Affinity and how does it influence pod placement in Kubernetes?
response: Affinity provides rules that give hints to the scheduler about where to place pods. It includes node affinity, which specifies conditions on a node's labels for placing pods there, and pod-to-pod affinity or anti-affinity, specifying relationships between different pods.
title: Glossary

instruction: What is the role of an Add-on in Kubernetes?
response: An add-on extends the functionality of Kubernetes. It can be installed to enhance various aspects such as monitoring or security, and some popular ones are listed under 'Installing addons'.
title: Glossary

instruction: What does ClusterRole represent in Kubernetes?
response: A cluster level, logical grouping of PolicyRules.
title: Rbac

instruction: How is a Role different from a ClusterRole in terms of scope and referencing method?
response: It's namespaced with effect only within its namespace. It can reference both Namespace-scoped Roles or global ClusterRoles, unlike the cluster-wide accessibility of ClusterRoles.
title: Rbac

instruction: What is a RoleBinding and how does it function in relation to roles?
response: A RoleBinding references a role but doesn't contain it. It adds who information via Subjects and namespace info by which namespace the binding exists, with effects confined within that specific namespace.
title: Rbac

instruction: Why do some telemetry agents communicate with Docker Engine?
response: Historically, Kubernetes was written to work specifically with Docker Engine. Some information relevant to telemetry is only available from Kubernetes components.
title: Migrating telemetry and security agents from dockershim

instruction: What has changed in the way some telemetry agents operate within a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Kubernetes gained the ability to support multiple runtimes, and now supports any runtime that is compatible with the container runtime interface. Some telemetry agents rely specifically on Docker Engine tooling.
title: Migrating telemetry and security agents from dockershim

instruction: What are some examples of commands used by an agent relying on Docker Engine?
response: An example includes running a command such as 'docker ps' or 'docker top' to list containers and processes, which is specific to Docker tooling.
title: Migrating telemetry and security agents from dockershim

instruction: What does ResourceQuota enforce per namespace?
response: Aggregate quota restrictions.
title: ResourceQuota

instruction: How can one view and set quotas in Kubernetes?
response: By using the types of resource quota, which includes hard limits for each named resource within a namespace scope selector.
title: ResourceQuota

instruction: What is ScopeSelectorOperator used to express in relation to ResourceQuota scopes?
response: Filters that must match objects tracked by the quota.
title: ResourceQuota

instruction: What is the purpose of SelfSubjectRulesReview in Kubernetes?
response: SelfSubjectRulesReview enumerates the set of actions a current user can perform within a namespace.
title: SelfSubjectRulesReview

instruction: Why should external systems avoid using SelfSubjectRulesReview for authorization decisions?
response: Using it raises confused deputy, cache lifetime/revocation, and correctness concerns.
title: SelfSubjectRulesReview

instruction: What are the recommended ways to defer Kubernetes API server-based authorization decisions instead of SelfSubjectRulesReview?
response: The SubjectAccessReview and LocalAccessReview APIs should be used for this purpose.
title: SelfSubjectRulesReview

instruction: Which CSI driver field must be set to true for the scheduler to consider volume size in its calculations, and what does this imply about node selection when a Pod requests storage using such volumes?
response: CSIDriverSpec.StorageCapacity; it implies that only nodes with enough available capacity will be considered suitable by the Kubernetes scheduler.
title: Storage Capacity

instruction: What are CSIStorageCapacity objects and how do they relate to storage accessibility across different nodes in a cluster?
response: CSIStorageCapacity objects contain information about one storage class's capacity, produced by the CSI driver. They define which specific Kubernetes nodes have access to that particular volume.
title: Storage Capacity

instruction: What does a CustomResourceDefinition (CRD) represent in Kubernetes?
response: A resource that should be exposed on the API server.
title: Apiextensions

instruction: How must the name of a CRD follow the format according to Apiextensions documentation?
response: In the format <.spec.name>.<.spec.group>.
title: Apiextensions

instruction: Where should one report an issue with this auto-generated page on Kubernetes, as suggested in the feedback section of Apiextensions documentation?
response: 'GitHub Repository' if reporting a problem or suggesting improvements.
title: Apiextensions

instruction: What is the primary function of Kubernetes Scheduler?
response: The scheduler's main role in Kubernetes is to ensure that Pods are matched with Nodes so they can run.
title: Kubernetes Scheduler

instruction: How does kube-scheduler select a node for running new or unscheduled pods?
response: It filters out nodes not meeting the specific requirements of the pod, scores feasible nodes using various functions and selects one with the highest score to execute the Pod. It then communicates this decision through binding.
title: Kubernetes Scheduler

instruction: What happens if no suitable node is found for a new or unscheduled pod?
response: If none of the Nodes are appropriate, the pod remains in an unscheduled state until the scheduler finds one that can accommodate it based on its requirements and constraints.
title: Kubernetes Scheduler

instruction: What is a common cause of 'Incompatible CNI versions' errors in pods?
response: 'Incompatible CNI versions' error occurs when the version specified for your container runtime does not match with that declared by plugins, specifically if config declares newer plugin versions than available.
title: Troubleshooting CNI plugin-related errors

instruction: How can one resolve a 'Failed to destroy network for sandbox' issue?
response: To fix this problem, edit the CNI configuration file and include missing version information of the plugin. This should allow successful pod stopping without leaving it in not-ready state.
title: Troubleshooting CNI plugin-related errors

instruction: What is recommended when dealing with Kubernetes issues related to container runtime versions?
response: It's advised that one verifies they are using or upgrading a tested compatible container runtime version for their specific Kubernetes setup, as per the documentation source k8s_docs.
title: Troubleshooting CNI plugin-related errors

instruction: How does Kubernetes handle communication between different nodes?
response: Kubernetes ensures that all pods can communicate with each other whether they are on the same node or on different nodes without using proxies.
title: Services, Load Balancing, and Networking

instruction: What is a Service in Kubernetes and how does it work with backend pods?
response: A service in Kubernetes provides a stable IP address or hostname for one or more backend pods. The individual pods making up the service can change over time, but clients interacting with the service remain unaffected.
title: Services, Load Balancing, and Networking

instruction: What is NetworkPolicy and what does it control in Kubernetes?
response: NetworkPolicy is a built-in Kubernetes API that allows you to control traffic between pods or between pods and the outside world, effectively managing network communication within your cluster.
title: Services, Load Balancing, and Networking

instruction: How can you apply a taint with key 'dedicated' and value 'special-user' to node 'foo'?
response: kubectl taint nodes foo dedicated=special-user:NoSchedule
title: kubectl taint

instruction: What command would remove the NoSchedule effect from a specific taint on node 'bar' with key 'dedicated' and value 'special-user'?
response: kubectl taint nodes bar dedicated=special0:NoSchedule
title: kubectl taint

instruction: How can you remove all the existing taints from a label selector on node labeled myLabel with key 'dedicated' and effect NoExecute using kubectl?
response: kubectl taint node -l myLabel=X dedicated:NoExecute-
title: kubectl taint

instruction: What does a NodeSelector represent in Kubernetes?
response: A node selector represents the union of the results of one or more label queries over a set of nodes; that is, it represents the OR of the selectors represented by the node selector terms.
title: NodeSelector

instruction: What are NodeSelectorTerms in Kubernetes?
response: NodeSelectorTerm array. Required. A list of node selector terms where each term corresponds to a label query over nodes, and these queries are ORed together as part of the overall selection.
title: NodeSelector

instruction: Where can you report issues related to this Kubernetes documentation page?
response: Open an issue in the GitHub Repository if reporting problems or suggesting improvements for auto-generated pages like NodeSelector, mentioning that fixes may need to be made elsewhere within the project.
title: NodeSelector

instruction: What does a ResourceClaim describe in Kubernetes?
response: A request for access to resources within the cluster, needed by workloads.
title: ResourceClaim

instruction: How is an accelerator device with specific properties requested using ResourceClaims?
response: By defining a DeviceClaim under spec in a ResourceClaim object.
title: ResourceClaim

instruction: What does the status stanza of a ResourceClaim track?
response: Whether the claim has been satisfied and what specific resources have been allocated.
title: ResourceClaim

instruction: What is Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) in Kubernetes?
response: Dynamic resource allocation (DRA) in Kubernetes is a feature that allows Pods to request and share resources, specifically devices like hardware accelerators.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation

instruction: How does DRA benefit the device categorization process within a cluster?
response: With Dynamic Resource Allocation, app operators can use device classes for centralized device categorization. This allows them to create optimized categories such as cost-optimized or high-performance devices suitable for various workloads.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation

instruction: How does DRA simplify the way Pods request resources?
response: Dynamic Resource Allocation simplifies resource requests by allowing app operators not to specify device quantities in their Pod's resource requests. Instead, they reference a claim that automatically applies the corresponding device configuration.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation

instruction: What are the two prominent examples of maintenance activities related to 'at rest storage' mentioned for Kubernetes?
response: The versioned schema of stored resources and encryption at rest.
title: Migrate Kubernetes Objects Using Storage Version Migration

instruction: Why is it necessary to ensure that a Resource has an integer resource version before running a migration, according to the documentation?
response: Because without this property, storage migrations will fail. This requirement does not apply if using aggregated APIs.
title: Migrate Kubernetes Objects Using Storage Version Migration

instruction: What is required for Kubernetes server in terms of its minimum version before attempting a migration as per the document's instructions?
response: The cluster must be at or later than version v1.30.
title: Migrate Kubernetes Objects Using Storage Version Migration

instruction: How do you pause a Kubernetes deployment using kubectl?
response: To pause a Kubernetes deployment, use the command `kubectl rollout pause deployment/nginx`.
title: kubectl rollout pause

instruction: What happens to a paused resource in terms of updates and reconciles by controllers?
response: Paused resources will continue their current state function; however, new updates won't affect the deployment as long as it remains paused. It also means that these resources are not managed or reconciled by any controller.
title: kubectl rollout pause

instruction: Can you resume a Kubernetes resource after pausing and what is required to do so?
response: Yes, you can resume a paused Kubernetes resource. To do this, use the `kubectl rollout resume` command followed by specifying which deployment or replication controller needs to be resumed.
title: kubectl rollout pause

instruction: What does setting 'spec.schedulingGroup' in the Pod manifest do?
response: 'Specifying a scheduling group allows you to link a Pod to a specific PodGroup object, which enables gang scheduling policies instead of treating each Pod independently.'
title: Scheduling Group

instruction: What happens if a referenced 'PodGroup' does not exist when setting the 'spec.schedulingGroup'?
response: 'If a referenced PodGroup is missing at the time you set spec.schedulingGroup, the Pod remains pending and will be rescheduled once the corresponding PodGroup object gets created.'
title: Scheduling Group

instruction: What are some of the policies that can be applied to 'PodGroups'?
response: 'Policies such as gang scheduling or basic policy-based independent scheduling can be defined for a specific PodGroup. The scheduler applies these group-level policies when setting up the pods.'
title: Scheduling Group

instruction: What is a ClusterTrustBundle in Kubernetes?
response: ClusterTrustBundle is a cluster-scoped container for X.509 trust anchors (root certificates).
title: ClusterTrustBundle

instruction: How can users access the contents of a ClusterTrustBundle if they only have namespace-level permissions?
response: Users with only namespace-level access to a cluster can read ClusterTrustBundles by impersonating a service account that has access.
title: ClusterTrustBundle

instruction: What is the purpose of admission control in relation to creating or modifying a signer's associated ClusterTrustBundle?
response: Admission control enforces that only users with permissions on the signer can create or modify the corresponding bundle.
title: ClusterTrustBundle

instruction: What is the purpose of using `kubeadm init phase preflight` command?
response: To execute pre-flight checks for kubeadm init.
title: kubeadm init phase

instruction: How can you specify a custom configuration file when running the `--config` flag with `preflight`?
response: By providing the path to your own config file, like 'kubeadm-config.yaml'.
title: kubeadm init phase

instruction: What does setting the `--ignore-preflight-errors=IsPrivilegedUser` flag do during a `preflight`?
response: It ignores errors related to 'IsPrivilegedUser', treating them as warnings instead.
title: kubeadm init phase

instruction: What is the purpose of Kubernetes Scheduler?
response: To decide on which node a pod should be placed.
title: Scheduling

instruction: What is the purpose of Kubernetes' 'TTL-after-finished' controller?
response: The TTL-after-finished controller provides a time to live mechanism to limit the lifetime of Job objects that have finished execution.
title: Automatic Cleanup for Finished Jobs

instruction: How does setting '.spec.ttlSecondsAfterFinished' in a Job manifest affect its cleanup process?
response: 'Specifying this field allows for automatic cascading removal of jobs after they finish, once the TTL has expired.
title: Automatic Cleanup for Finished Jobs

instruction: Can Kubernetes 'TTL-after-finished' controller be used to set different cleanup times based on job status or labels?

instruction: What does the Ingress API being frozen imply for its development and changes?
response: The Ingress API will have no further changes or updates made to it.

instruction: What does AKS Application Gateway Ingress Controller do and which service does it configure for Azure?

response: The size of the allocations should correspond to planned Pod and Service counts, using private ranges typically chosen by cluster operators. For example: `10.244.0.0/16` for IPv4 pods and a global unicast address block from within `2000::/3`.

instruction: How can I set a scheduling policy for Kubernetes versions before v1.23?

instruction: What should I use instead of the scheduling policy to achieve similar behavior in Kubernetes v1.23 or later?
response: Use Scheduler Configuration with `--scheduler-configuration <file>` flag as a replacement for setting up scheduler policies.

instruction: What is the primary issue with standard Kubernetes scheduler when dealing with multiple concurrent workloads?

instruction: How does PodGroup scheduling differ from individual pod scheduling in terms of approach and execution time?
response: PodGroup treats a collection of pending Pods as one unit, evaluating feasibility for the entire group simultaneously before moving to binding.

instruction: What are the prerequisites required on the cluster side to enable and use PodGroup scheduling?

instruction: What is a rolling update in Kubernetes and how does it ensure application availability?

instruction: What types of resources does Kubernetes clean up through garbage collection?

instruction: How does Kubernetes use owner references in managing resources?

instruction: What is the difference between ownership in Kubernetes and labels/selectors?

instruction: What is horizontal manual scaling for a deployment in Kubernetes, and how do you perform it?
response: Horizontal Manual Scaling involves manually adjusting the number of Pods running from within the Deployment's YAML configuration.
title: Run Applications

instruction: How can I update my application without causing downtime in Kubernetes?
response: Use a Rolling Update strategy with your Deployments to ensure zero-downtime updates by gradually replacing old Pods with new ones.
title: Run Applications

instruction: What are some methods and tools for setting up a production Kubernetes cluster?
response: kubeadm, Cluster API (a sub-project of Kubernetes), kops, and Kubespray.
title: Installing Kubernetes with deployment tools

instruction: For what purpose was the 'Update Link of Inventory in Install k8s doc' issue created?
response: To update a link related to inventory in an installation guide for Kubernetes as per change 7692020f50.
title: Installing Kubernetes with deployment tools

instruction: Where can one find tutorials, best practices, and community information regarding kops?
response: On the 'kOps' website
title: Installing Kubernetes with deployment tools

instruction: What does the Core componentStatus hold in Kubernetes?
response: The cluster validation info.
title: Core

instruction: Why are Endpoints considered deprecated and what should be used instead for complete information about Service endpoints?
response: Endpoints is a legacy API, use discoveryv1.EndpointSlice for more comprehensive endpoint details.
title: Core

instruction: What does an Event in Kubernetes represent and how reliable should it be treated by consumers?
response: An event report of something happening in the cluster with limited retention time; events are informative, best-effort data and not relied upon for consistent triggers.
title: Core

instruction: What does kube-controller-manager do within a Kubernetes system?
response: The Kubernetes controller manager is a daemon that embeds core control loops, regulating the state of the system towards desired states.
title: kube-controller-manager

instruction: In what scenarios are controllers like replication and endpoints typically used in Kubernetes applications involving robotics or automation?
response: Controllers such as replication and endpoints are often utilized for maintaining a stable number of pods, ensuring that the desired state matches the actual cluster configuration.
title: kube-controller-manager

instruction: How does kube-controller-manager handle volume attach detach reconciliation?
response: The controller manager has an option --attach-detach-reconcile-sync-period which sets a wait time between syncing the state of volumes with their corresponding pods.
title: kube-controller-manager

instruction: What is the purpose of Kubernetes API?
response: Kubernetes' API serves functionality through a RESTful interface and stores the state of the cluster.
title: Kubernetes API

instruction: How can users interact with the core Kubernetes API directly or via tools like kubectl?
response: Users can manage configuration in a declarative way, making changes to resources through RESTful calls to the API using direct interaction or kubectl.
title: Kubernetes API

instruction: Can custom objects be supported by Kubernetes core API and if so, how?
response: Yes, the core Kubernetes API is flexible enough to support extensions for custom resources.
title: Kubernetes API

instruction: What does CSINode hold information about in a Kubernetes environment?
response: All CSI drivers installed on a node.
title: CSINode

instruction: How is the CSINode object populated for each driver by default without direct creation from the driver itself?
response: The kubelet will automatically populate it as part of plugin registration using the node-driver-registrar sidecar container.
title: CSINode

instruction: What does a missing CSINode object indicate about CSI drivers or Kubelet version on a node?
response: Either there are no available CSI Drivers, or the Kubelet version is too low to create this object.
title: CSINode

instruction: What is the recommended endpoint for OpenTelemetry tracing clients?
response: Unset, and localhost:4317
title: kube-apiserver Configuration (v1alpha1)

instruction: How does JWT authenticator verify a raw ID token in Kubernetes authentication configuration?
response: It attempts to parse the incoming token, verifies it's been signed by the configured issuer using OIDC discovery.
title: kube-apiserver Configuration (v1alpha1)

instruction: What happens if no samplingRatePerMillion is set for OpenTelemetry tracing clients?
response: If unset, sampler respects its parent span's sampling rate, but otherwise never samples.
title: kube-apiserver Configuration (v1alpha1)

instruction: What is required to access a Kubernetes API using SSH and SOCKS5 proxy?
response: A working Kubernetes cluster, kubectl command-line tool configured for the cluster communication, an SSH client software with running SSH service on the remote server.
title: Use a SOCKS5 Proxy to Access the Kubernetes API

instruction: What is recommended regarding nodes in your Kubernetes cluster when setting up a SOCKS5 proxy?
response: At least two non-control plane host nodes are required for this setup to work effectively.
title: Use a SOCKS5 Proxy to Access the Kubernetes API

instruction: How does the traffic flow from local client machine through SSH and SOCKS5 tunnel towards accessing Kubernetes API on remote server?
response: Local HTTPs traffic is directed firstly into a Local SSH SOCKS5 proxy, then tunnelled over SSH to an SSH server (sshd), which in turn routes the request directly to service1 representing access point for Kubernetes API.
title: Use a SOCKS5 Proxy to Access the Kubernetes API

instruction: What does the kubectl command do in Kubernetes?
response: Kubernetes provides a command line tool for communicating with a cluster's control plane.
title: Command line tool (kubectl)

instruction: Where can you find configuration files used by kubectl to communicate with your Kubernetes cluster?
response: kubectl looks for a file named 'config' in the '$HOME/.kube' directory.
title: Command line tool (kubectl)

instruction: How do I specify multiple resource types individually when using kubectl commands on more than one type of resources, such as pods and services?
response: To group different resource types separately for a command like 'get', use the format: TYPE1/name1 TYPE2/name2.
title: Command line tool (kubectl)

instruction: How can a namespace be created to isolate resources being used?
response: A namespace named 'constraints-cpu-example' is created using kubectl create namespace constraints-cpu-example.
title: Configure Minimum and Maximum CPU Constraints for a Namespace

instruction: What command would you use to apply the LimitRange configuration file and specify a namespace for it in Kubernetes?
response: The following command applies the 'admin/resource/cpu-constraints.yaml' manifest into the specified namespace: kubectl apply -f https://k8s.io/examples/admin/resource/cpu-constraints.yaml --namespace constraints-cpu-example.
title: Configure Minimum and Maximum CPU Constraints for a Namespace

instruction: What are the minimum and maximum CPU resource limits set for containers in this LimitRange example?
response: The Minimum is '200m' CPU resources, while the Maximum allowed limit is '800m'.
title: Configure Minimum and Maximum CPU Constraints for a Namespace

instruction: What does the 'kubeadm version' command do?
response: This command prints the version of kubeadm.
title: kubeadm version

instruction: Which flags can be used with the 'kubeadm version' command to specify output format and root filesystem path for chrooting into, respectively?
response: -o string --output flag specifies the output format; available options are 'yaml', 'json' and 'short'. The -r or --rootfs string option provides the path to the real host root filesystem.
title: kubeadm version

instruction: Where can one provide feedback on this page, report a problem with it, or suggest an improvement?
response: Feedback is appreciated by asking specific questions about how to use Kubernetes on Stack Overflow. Report problems or suggestions for improvements should be made in the GitHub Repository.
title: kubeadm version

instruction: What version of Kubernetes must your server be at or later than when using CoreDNS?
response: Kubernetes version v1.9.
title: Using CoreDNS for Service Discovery

instruction: How can you migrate to CoreDNS in a cluster that was previously running kube-dns with Kubeadm 1.21 and later versions of Kubernetes?
response: By using Kubeadm, which generates the CoreDNS configuration ('Corefile') based on the existing 'kube-dns' ConfigMap.
title: Using CoreDNS for Service Discovery

instruction: What is a recommended way to install or replace kube-dns with CoreDNS in an existing Kubernetes cluster?
response: By using tools like Kubeadm that will deploy and upgrade the cluster for you, if available. Otherwise, manual deployment as per documentation on the CoreDNS website.
title: Using CoreDNS for Service Discovery

instruction: What is the role of kube-scheduler in Kubernetes?
response: The scheduler's responsibility is to place Pods on Nodes within a cluster.
title: Scheduler Performance Tuning

instruction: How does setting 'percentageOfNodesToScore' affect the behavior of kube-scheduler?
response: It determines a threshold for scheduling nodes, balancing between latency and accuracy in placement decisions. A value above 100 is treated as if it were set to 100.
title: Scheduler Performance Tuning

instruction: What should be done after editing the kube-scheduler configuration file?
response: Restart the scheduler and verify its health by running 'kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep kube-scheduler'.
title: Scheduler Performance Tuning

instruction: What does the `apiVersion` field in a Pod specification represent?
response: APIVersion defines the versioned schema of this representation of an object.
title: Pod

instruction: In Kubernetes, what is meant by 'static pods' as mentioned on page 597?
response: Static Pods are a concept where certain containers within a Pod may be statically assigned to specific nodes or hosts for consistent scheduling.
title: Pod

instruction: How does the `Pod` resource in Kubernetes facilitate communication between its contained components, as discussed on page 598?
response: The document suggests that Pods are designed to allow containers within them to share resources and communicate with each other directly.
title: Pod

instruction: How do you use 'kubectl rollout status' with a deployment?
response: To watch the rollout status of a Deployment, run `kubectl rollout status deployment/nginx`.
title: kubectl rollout status

instruction: What flags can be used to specify which revision 'kubectl rollout status' should monitor?
response: '--revision=N', where N is the specific revision number you want to watch for, or use `--watch` with a value of false.
title: kubectl rollout status

instruction: How can multiple groups be specified when using 'kubectl rollout status'?
response: '--as-group strings', this flag allows specifying multiple groups by repeating it as necessary.
title: kubectl rollout status

instruction: What are ExternalMetricValue and ExternalMetricValueList used for in Kubernetes?
response: ExternalMetricValue is a metric value with labels, while ExternalMetricValueList is a list of these values.
title: Kubernetes External Metrics (v1beta1)

instruction: Which API version does the external metrics (v1beta1) use and what are its required fields?
response: The v1beta1 uses 'external.metrics.k8s.io/v1beta1' apiVersion, with metricName as a string that is Required, timestamp also Required which indicates the time at which metrics were produced, window of type int64 indicating from when these metrics are calculated and value required field.
title: Kubernetes External Metrics (v1beta1)

instruction: How can you identify multiple values for one Kubernetes external metric?
response: Multiple values for a single metric in Kubernetes External Metrics (v1beta1) API can be identified by different sets of labels attached to each value.
title: Kubernetes External Metrics (v1beta1)

instruction: How do you enable process namespace sharing for a pod in Kubernetes?
response: Process namespace sharing is enabled using the 'shareProcessNamespace' field of 'spec' for a Pod.
title: Share Process Namespace between Containers in a Pod

instruction: What command can be used to attach an interactive shell inside one container and run commands within another container, while also enabling process tracing between them in Kubernetes?
response: The following kubectl exec command is used: `kubectl exec -it nginx -c shell -- /bin/sh`.
title: Share Process Namespace between Containers in a Pod

instruction: How can you signal processes running inside other containers of the same pod to perform an action, such as restarting a worker process in Nginx?
response: You can send signals like SIGHUP between container processes within the same Pod using commands run from one container. For example: `kill -SIGHUP $(pidof nginx)`.
title: Share Process Namespace between Containers in a Pod

instruction: What is the purpose of using 'kubectl uncordon' in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Mark node as schedulable.
title: kubectl uncordon

instruction: How can you specify which nodes to mark as schedulable when using kubectl uncordon with the --selector option?
response: By providing a label query, such as -l key1=value1,key2=value2.
title: kubectl uncordon

instruction: What does setting the '--as-user' flag to an UID accomplish when using kubectl uncordon?
response: Impersonates a user with that specific User ID for the operation, which could be either regular or service account.
title: kubectl uncordon

instruction: How can all Kubernetes certificates be renewed using the command line tool provided in the documentation?
response: By running `kubeadm certs renew all` or specifying individual ones to renew selectively with additional options, as detailed in 'Manual certificate renewal'.
title: kubeadm certs

instruction: What is indicated by `--rootfs string` option and where can it be used?
response: '--rootfs string' specifies the path to the real host root filesystem for kubeadm, which chroots into this provided path when using related commands.
title: kubeadm certs

instruction: What does a ControllerRevision represent and how can it be updated?
response: ControllerRevision represents an immutable snapshot of state data for clients, which are responsible for serializing/deserializing objects containing their internal state. It cannot be updated once created.
title: Apps

instruction: What is the primary purpose of a StatefulSet in Kubernetes?
response: A StatefulSet represents pods with consistent identities, ensuring that network identity always maps to the same storage identity through VolumeClaims.
title: Apps

instruction: How does DaemonSet differ from Deployment and ReplicaSet in Kubernetes?
response: DaemonSet manages daemonset configurations, while Deployments enable declarative updates for Pods and ReplicaSets. A ReplicaSet ensures a specified number of pod replicas are running at any given time.
title: Apps

instruction: What type of volumes are implemented with a tmpfs filesystem on Linux nodes in Kubernetes?
response: Memory-backed volumes such as secret volume mounts and emptyDir with medium: Memory.
title: Security For Linux Nodes

instruction: Why might data from memory-backed volumes be written to persistent storage despite the use of swap, especially on older Linux kernels?
response: Because if swap is configured and an older kernel (or a current one with unsupported Kubernetes configuration) is used.
title: Security For Linux Nodes

instruction: What option can be set to prevent data from memory-backed volumes on Linux nodes in Kubernetes being written to persistent storage when using swap?
response: The noswap kernel option, which is officially supported starting with version 6.3 of the Linux kernel.
title: Security For Linux Nodes

instruction: What does the `attacher` field in the VolumeAttachmentSpec indicate?
response: The name of the volume driver that handles this request.
title: VolumeAttachment

instruction: How is a VolumeAttachmentStatus populated and by whom within Kubernetes infrastructure?
response: By the entity completing the attach or detach operation, i.e., the external-attacher.
title: VolumeAttachment

instruction: What does `VolumeAttachmentSpec` represent in a VolumeAttachment object and how is it populated within Kubernetes?
response: 'VolumeAttachmentSpec' represents the specification of a VolumeAttachment request. It is non-namespaced, meaning that this definition applies across all namespaces.
title: VolumeAttachment

instruction: How can I forcefully delete resources in Kubernetes using kubectl?
response: To forcefully delete resources, specify the --force flag with your deletion command.
title: kubectl delete

instruction: What could be a potential risk of forcibly deleting pods without waiting for their processes to terminate in Kubernetes?
response: Force-deleting pods may result in data corruption or inconsistency if the application uses shared storage, communicates with remote APIs using pod names as identifiers, and does not handle multiple copies of a running pod.
title: kubectl delete

instruction: What happens to resources that do not support graceful deletion when I use kubectl delete?
response: If the resource doesn't support graceful deletion, using --grace-period is ignored and force deleting may be used.
title: kubectl delete

instruction: What is the primary function of the Kubernetes scheduler?
response: The Kubernetes scheduler assigns Pods to Nodes based on constraints and available resources.
title: kube-scheduler

instruction: How does kube-scheduler determine a suitable Node for binding a Pod if multiple valid nodes are present?
response: Multiple different schedulers may be used within a cluster, but the text doesn't provide specific details on how they rank or bind to a suitable node when several options exist.
title: kube-scheduler

instruction: What happens if authentication-kubeconfig is empty and no client CA lookup in the cluster?
response: If the authentication-kubeconfig file is empty, all token requests are considered anonymous without looking up a missing configuration from the cluster.
title: kube-scheduler

instruction: What are API groups and their versions as summarized by k8s_docs?
response: API Groups and their versions include admissionregistration, apiextensions, apps (v1), authentication, authorization (both v1), autoscaling (v2, v1), batch (v1), certificates (v1, v1beta1, v1alpha1), coordination (v1, v1beta1, v1alpha2), core (v1), discovery (v1), events (v1), flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io (v1), internal.apiserver.k8s.io (v1alpha1), networking (v1, v1beta1), node (v1), policy (v1), rbac.authorization.k8s.io (v1), resource (v1, v1beta2, v1beta1, v1alpha3), scheduling (v1, v1alpha2), storage (v1, v1beta1) and storagemigration (v1beta1).
title: API Groups

instruction: Which API group is responsible for managing pod disruption budgets?
response: The 'scheduling' API group with versions 'v1, v1alpha2'. Note that this information was last modified on May 13, 2026.
title: API Groups

instruction: What is the purpose of feedback mechanisms mentioned in k8s_docs?
response: The provided text does not explicitly mention a specific mechanism for providing feedback. However, it suggests that if someone finds this page helpful or has questions about using Kubernetes, they should ask on Stack Overflow and report issues with GitHub Repository.
title: API Groups

instruction: What is Topology-Aware Scheduling (TAS) in Kubernetes?
response: Topology-Aware Scheduling (TAS) is a placement scheduling algorithm that finds the optimal pod placement within the same topology domain, ensuring collocation of all pods.
title: Topology-Aware Workload Scheduling

instruction: How does TopologyPlacement plugin contribute to TAS?
response: The TopologyPlacement plugin generates candidate placements by grouping nodes based on distinct values of a requested topology key defined in the PodGroup, contributing to optimal pod collocation within the same domain.
title: Topology-Aware Workload Scheduling

instruction: How can users customize weights for NodeResourcesFit and PodGroupPodsCount plugins?
response: Users can adjust these plugin weights or resource weights used in bin-packing strategy by modifying their KubeSchedulerConfiguration. For example, they could set different default values like 2 for one plugin and 1 for another.
title: Topology-Aware Workload Scheduling

instruction: What is a StorageVersion in Kubernetes?
response: A storage version of a specific resource.
title: StorageVersion

instruction: How does the commonEncodingVersion field function within the context of multiple API server instances sharing one encoding storage version?
response: When all API server instances share the same storage version, this field is set to that shared version. If they don't have a shared version, it remains empty.
title: StorageVersion

instruction: What does the StorageVersionSpec represent in Kubernetes and how should servers handle its emptiness?
response: StorageVersionSpec represents an empty spec for compliance with Kubernetes API style. Servers may leave this field as is, since it's intentionally designed to be empty.
title: StorageVersion

instruction: What is the hostname of a Container in Kubernetes?
response: The hostname of a Container is the name of the Pod it's running on.
title: Container Environment

instruction: How can I access user-defined environment variables from within a container?
response: User-defined environment variables are available to Containers through downward API, as well as any statically defined in the image or inherited from Pod definition.
title: Container Environment

instruction: How can I find out which services were running when my Container was created?
response: A list of all services that were running at the time a Container was created is available as environment variables, limited to services within the same namespace and including dedicated IP addresses if DNS addon is enabled.
title: Container Environment

instruction: What does PriorityClass in Kubernetes define?
response: Mapping from a priority class name to the priority integer value.
title: PriorityClass

instruction: How is globalDefault used within PriorityClasses and what happens if more than one has it set as true?
response: GlobalDefault specifies whether this PriorityClass should be considered default for pods without any. If multiple have GlobalDefault=true, the smallest value becomes the default priority.
title: PriorityClass

instruction: What is preemptionPolicy in Kubernetes and what are its possible values?
response: 'PreemptLowerPriority' means that a higher-priority pod can preempt lower ones. The other option not mentioned here but implied by the context would be 'Never', where no such behavior is allowed.
title: PriorityClass

instruction: How does Kubernetes handle voluntary and involuntary disruptions of Pods?
response: Voluntary disruptions are started intentionally by application owners or cluster administrators, while involuntary ones can be triggered by unavoidable issues like Nodes running out of resources.
title: Scheduling, Preemption and Eviction

instruction: What is the purpose of Pod Priority and Preemption in Kubernetes?
response: Pod Priority and Preemption ensures that higher-priority Pods are scheduled on nodes before lower priority ones, potentially terminating lower priority Pods to make room.
title: Scheduling, Preemption and Eviction

instruction: How does a StatefulSet differ from Deployments regarding Pod management and identity within a cluster?
response: A StatefulSet manages one or more Pods where the Pods rely on having distinct identities, unlike in Deployments where any Pod can be interchangeable.
title: Workload Management

instruction: What is a DaemonSet and when would you use it within Kubernetes?
response: A DaemonSet defines Pods that provide node-level services like drivers for local storage systems. It's used when the service needs to run on each node where it’s useful.
title: Workload Management

instruction: What is required before you can safely drain a node in Kubernetes?
response: You need to have met certain prerequisites, such as not requiring your applications to be highly available during the node drain or having read about and configured PodDisruptionBudgets for necessary workloads.
title: Safely Drain a Node

instruction: What is a recommended practice if availability of workloads running on drained nodes must remain uninterrupted?
response: It's recommended to configure a PodDisruptionBudget with an 'AlwaysAllow' Unhealthy Pod Eviction Policy before proceeding.
title: Safely Drain a Node

instruction: What does the `kubectl drain` command do in Kubernetes?
response: 'kubectl drain' safely evicts all of your pods from a node, respecting any defined PodDisruptionBudgets and desired graceful termination periods.
title: Safely Drain a Node

instruction: What is Volume Health Monitoring and which components implement this feature?
response: Volume Health Monitoring allows CSI Drivers to detect abnormal conditions on volumes from the underlying storage systems, reporting them as events. It's implemented by an External Health Monitor controller and kubelet.
title: Volume Health Monitoring

instruction: How does Volume Health Monitoring report node failure?
response: The feature reports a pod event on its associated PVC when the external health monitor detects a node failure, indicating that pods using this PVC are on failed nodes.
title: Volume Health Monitoring

instruction: What metrics does Kubelet expose for Volume Health Monitoring and what do they indicate?
response: Kubelet exposes the metric kubelet_volume_stats_health_status_abnormal, which includes labels namespace and persistentvolumeclaim. The count is 1 if unhealthy or 0 for healthy volume status.
title: Volume Health Monitoring

instruction: Where should I report security issues related to Kubernetes?
response: Report security issues on GitHub Issues and suggest improvements or problems there.
title: Kubernetes Issue Tracker

instruction: How can I stay informed about known CVEs for Kubernetes?
response: Refer to the official list of known CVEs announced by the Security Response Committee, which is fetched using a JSON feed (cafe6d258c).
title: Kubernetes Issue Tracker

instruction: What should I do if someone finds an issue with Kubernetes that isn't listed as a CVE?
response: If you find such issues, open them on GitHub Repository to report the problem or suggest improvements.

response: To list recent events in the default namespace, use `kubectl events`.

response: 'kubectl events --for pod/web-pod-13je7 --watch --types=Warning,Normal'
title: kubectl events

instruction: How can I output the list of recent kubectl events in YAML format while suppressing headers?
response: 'kubectl events -oyaml --no-headers
title: kubectl events

instruction: What is the default expiration time for certificates generated by kubeadm?
response: Certificates generated by kubeadm expire after one year.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm

instruction: Where should custom certificates be placed if you want to override the default behavior of kubeadm in generating them?
response: Custom certificates must be placed in whatever directory is specified by the --cert-dir flag or the certificatesDir field of kubeadm's ClusterConfiguration, which defaults to /etc/kubernetes/pki.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm

instruction: What encryption algorithms can you choose when creating public and private keys with Kubeadm?
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm

instruction: How can you manually scale a Deployment horizontally in Kubernetes?
response: By changing its replica count using the kubectl command.
title: Horizontal Manual Scaling for a Deployment

instruction: What is one objective of scaling up a Deployment to handle more traffic?
response: To increase resource allocation for handling increased load, which can be done by setting higher replica counts.
title: Horizontal Manual Scaling for a Deployment

instruction: Which tool or method should you use if your goal is not just practice but also applying horizontal manual scaling in Kubernetes with an existing Deployment?
response: Use the kubectl apply command from a provided YAML file to create and then scale up an nginx Deployment.
title: Horizontal Manual Scaling for a Deployment

instruction: What is a CertificateSigningRequest in Kubernetes and what purpose does it serve?
response: CertificateSigningRequest objects provide a mechanism to obtain x509 certificates by submitting a certificate signing request, which can be asynchronously approved and issued. They are used for authenticating kubelets to the kube-apiserver.
title: CertificateSigningRequest

instruction: How do Kubelets use CertificateSigningRequest objects?
response: Kubelets utilize this API primarily to procure client certificates that enable them to securely authenticate with the kube-apiserver, using specific signerNames like 'kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client-kubelet'.
title: CertificateSigningRequest

instruction: Can CertificateSigningRequest objects be used for purposes other than obtaining certificates from Kubernetes?
response: Yes, the API can also facilitate acquiring client certificates to authenticate with kube-apiserver using different signerNames or even request certs from custom non-Kubernetes signers.
title: CertificateSigningRequest

instruction: What is a strategic merge patch and when would you use it in Kubernetes?
response: A strategic merge patch updates an API object by combining the differences between the current state of the object and its desired new state. It's used for objects that support merging, like Deployments.
title: Update API Objects in Place Using kubectl patch

instruction: How can you update a Kubernetes Pod to run two containers instead of one?
response: To add another container to an existing pod in Kubernetes without recreating it, use the kubectl edit command or apply changes using `kubectl patch`.
title: Update API Objects in Place Using kubectl patch

instruction: What is required before you begin updating a Deployment with strategic merge patches?
response: Before beginning to update a deployment in Kubernetes, ensure that there's an existing cluster and the kubectl command-line tool is configured for communication.
title: Update API Objects in Place Using kubectl patch

instruction: What does the Seccomp feature do in Kubernetes?
response: Seccomp stands for secure computing mode and it restricts a process's privileges by sandboxing its ability to make calls from userspace into the kernel.
title: Restrict a Container's Syscalls with seccomp

instruction: How can seccomp profiles be applied to containers within a Kubernetes Pod?
response: Seccomp profiles loaded onto a node in Kubernetes are automatically applied to your Pods and containers, restricting their syscalls according to the profile.
title: Restrict a Container's Syscalls with seccomp

instruction: Can you apply seccomp profiles using privileged: true set on container?
response: No, it is not possible to apply a seccomp profile when running a container with privileges set as 'privileged: true' in Kubernetes.
title: Restrict a Container's Syscalls with seccomp

response: To learn how to generate certificates, see Certificates in k8s_docs.
title: Certificates

instruction: How can you list all current namespaces in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Use the command kubectl get namespaces
title: Share a Cluster with Namespaces

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'kube-node-lease' namespace and what objects does it hold?
response: 'Kube-node-lease' holds Lease objects associated with each node, allowing kubelet to send heartbeats for detecting node failure.
title: Share a Cluster with Namespaces

instruction: What are the default resource limits set on pods in the 'default' namespace?
response: The CPU limit is set to 100 millicpu, and there seems to be a memory specification as well but it wasn't provided.
title: Share a Cluster with Namespaces

instruction: How does Kubernetes ensure secure communication between nodes or pods and the API server?
response: Kubernetes ensures secured communication by using a 'hub-and-spoke' API pattern where all requests terminate at the API server, which listens for remote connections on a secure HTTPS port with client authentication enabled.
title: Communication between Nodes and the Control Plane

instruction: What role does TLS play in node to control plane communications?
response: TLS is used by nodes and pods when connecting to the API server, which automatically injects public root certificate and a valid bearer token into the pod upon instantiation.
title: Communication between Nodes and the Control Plane

instruction: What are some best practices for hardening network configurations in Kubernetes?
response: Best practices include using client certificates provided to kubelet, ensuring that one or more forms of authorization are enabled (especially if anonymous requests or service account tokens are allowed), and provisioning nodes with the public root certificate.
title: Communication between Nodes and the Control Plane

instruction: What does MatchCondition represent in Kubernetes?
response: MatchCondition represents a condition which must be fulfilled for a request to be sent to a webhook.

instruction: In the context of CEL, what is 'object' and how can it vary based on HTTP method used in an admission request?

instruction: How does a misconfigured scheduler potentially aid an attacker?

instruction: How do you resume a paused deployment using kubectl rollout command?

instruction: What does RuntimeClass define in Kubernetes?
response: RuntimeClass defines a class of container runtime supported in the cluster.

instruction: How is the appropriate container runtime determined for running containers within a pod according to the document?
response: The RuntimeClass, which specifies the underlying runtime and configuration that will be used by the CRI implementation based on user or provisioner-defined handlers.

instruction: What is required of the 'handler' field in a RuntimeClass definition?
response: 'Handler' must specify the container runtime handler, conform to DNS label requirements and be immutable. It indicates which CRI implementation will handle pods for that class.

instruction: What is the purpose of kubectl proxy and what protocol does client to proxy use?
response: The kubectl proxy proxies from a localhost address to the Kubernetes apiserver, adds authentication headers, and uses HTTP. The client to proxy also uses HTTPS.
title: Proxies in Kubernetes

instruction: How does the apiserver proxy function in terms of connectivity and protocol?
response: The apiserver proxy is a bastion built into the apiserver that proxies user outside of the cluster to reach Node, Pod, or Service using HTTPS (or http if configured). It can use either HTTP or HTTPS based on available information.

instruction: What is a kubeconfig file used for in Kubernetes?
response: A kubeconfig file organizes information about clusters, users, namespaces, and authentication mechanisms.

instruction: Where does the default `kubectl` look for configuration files on a system using Bash scripting conventions?

instruction: How can you specify an alternative kubeconfig file to be used by `kubectl` when multiple are available?

instruction: What are the different purposes for using Kubernetes volumes?
response: Populating configuration files based on ConfigMap or Secret, providing temporary scratch space, sharing filesystem between containers in a Pod, sharing filesystem between Pods across nodes, durably storing data to persist beyond pod restarts/replacements, passing configuration information of the container's namespace, and read-only access from another image.
title: Volumes

instruction: Why are volumes important for non-trivial applications running in containers?
response: On-disk files within a container are ephemeral. Without persistent storage, data created or modified during the lifetime of the container is lost after crashes.
title: Volumes

instruction: How does Kubernetes solve problems related to shared filesystem and durable data persistence?
response: Kubernetes solves these issues through its volume abstraction. It allows sharing files between containers in a Pod, even across different nodes (for persistent storage), while also providing various ephemeral volumes for temporary or configuration-specific needs.
title: Volumes

instruction: What is the purpose of using Finalizers in Kubernetes?
response: Finalizers are used to control garbage collection by alerting controllers to perform specific cleanup tasks before deleting a target resource.
title: Finalizers

instruction: How does Kubernetes handle an object with finalizers when it receives a delete request for that same object again?
response: Kubernetes will not remove the object until all items are removed from its metadata.finalizers field, even if another deletion attempt is made.
title: Finalizers

instruction: What happens to an object in Kubernetes after finalizers have been successfully executed and emptied out?
response: Once the controller has completed executing tasks specified by the finalizers (if any), it removes them from the target resource. When metadata.finalizers field is empty, Kubernetes considers deletion complete.
title: Finalizers

instruction: What are the requirements for Kubernetes nodes to support pre-allocated huge pages?
response: Nodes must allocate huge pages in advance.

instruction: How can a node report its capacity of allocated huge pages, and what is an example configuration line that allocates specific sizes of 1 GiB and 2 MiB huge pages for multiple counts on boot time?
response: The nodes will automatically discover and report all huge page resources as schedulable. An example GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX parameter to allocate these is: 'hugepagesz=1G hugepages=2 hugepagesz=2M hugepages=512'.
title: Manage HugePages

instruction: What are the implications of requesting multiple sizes of huge pages in a single pod, and what must be done if dynamic allocation occurs?
response: A pod may consume multiple huge page sizes but needs to use 'medium: HugePages-<size>'. If dynamically allocated on boot time, the Kubelet should be restarted for new allocations to reflect.
title: Manage HugePages

instruction: What are custom resources in Kubernetes and how can they be extended?

instruction: How does an aggregation layer facilitate specialized implementations in Kubernetes?
response: The aggregation layer acts as a proxy between the main API server and custom APIs, allowing users to write their own API servers for providing tailored handling of specific resource types.
