title: Changing The Kubernetes Package Repository

instruction: What should you do if the Kubernetes package repositories are not being used?
response: It is strongly recommended to migrate to the community-owned repository as described in a subsequent section (not provided).
title: Changing The Kubernetes Package Repository

instruction: What is a CustomResourceDefinition (CRD) in Kubernetes?
response: A custom resource definition extends the Kubernetes API by creating new RESTful resources.
title: Extend the Kubernetes API with CustomResourceDefinitions

instruction: How can you create and install a CRD into your cluster using kubectl command-line tool, as per the documentation provided on 'Extend the Kubernetes API with CustomResourceDefinitions'?
response: Create a YAML file defining the custom resource (CR) specification under `apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1`, then run `kubectl create -f resourcedefinition.yaml`.
title: Extend the Kubernetes API with CustomResourceDefinitions

instruction: What happens to all namespaced CRD objects when a namespace is deleted in Kubernetes?
response: Deleting a namespace removes all custom resources, including those defined by CustomResourceDefinitions.
title: Extend the Kubernetes API with CustomResourceDefinitions

instruction: What are labels in Kubernetes?
response: Labels are key/value pairs that can be attached to objects such as Pods for identification and organization.
title: Labels and Selectors

instruction: How do you attach a label with the value 'stable' to indicate release status, using kubectl command?
response: To attach a label indicating 'release' is stable, use `kubectl label pods mypod --overwrite -n=my-namespace key1/value1`.
title: Labels and Selectors

instruction: What should be used to record non-identifying information in Kubernetes objects?
response: Non-identifying information should be recorded using annotations, not labels.
title: Labels and Selectors

instruction: How does Kubeadm bootstrap a Kubernetes control-plane node?
response: Kubeadm initializes the cluster by setting up essential components like etcd, kube-apiserver, controller manager, scheduler on one or more nodes.
title: Kubeadm

instruction: What is required to join a Kubernetes worker node using Kubeadm?
response: The 'kubeadm join' command needs the master server address and token for joining a worker node to the cluster.
title: Kubeadm

instruction: How can I revert any changes made by kubeadm init or join on my host system using Kubeadm?
response: To revert, use 'kubeadm reset' which removes all Kubernetes-related configurations and data from the current node.
title: Kubeadm

instruction: What does the kubectl cluster-info dump command do by default?
response: Dumps everything to stdout.
title: kubectl cluster-info dump

instruction: How can you specify a directory for output when using the --output-directory flag with kubectl cluster-info dump?
response: Specify it as follows: `kubectl cluster-info dump --output-directory=/path/to/cluster-state`.
title: kubectl cluster-info dump

instruction: What happens if both --all-namespaces and a specific list of namespaces are provided to the kubectl cluster-info dump command?
response: The namespace specified in the `--namespaces` flag takes precedence over `--all-namespaces`.
title: kubectl cluster-info dump

instruction: What is the Pod Security Admission Controller in Kubernetes?
response: 'Pod Security Admission Controller' refers to a built-in controller which enforces 'Pod Security Standards.'
title: Pod Security Admission

instruction: How are pod security restrictions applied within namespaces when using Pod Security admission control?
response: When the feature is enabled or webhook installed, namespace configurations can be set through labels that define desired Pod Security Standard levels.
title: Pod Security Admission

instruction: What happens if a policy violation occurs in 'enforce' mode of pod security during creation within Kubernetes namespaces?
response: 'Policy violations will cause the pod to be rejected.'
title: Pod Security Admission

instruction: What is the primary role of kubelet in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The kubelet acts as the 'node agent' that registers nodes with the apiserver and ensures containers described by PodSpecs are running.
title: kubelet

instruction: How can one override the hostname used for node registration when using kubelet on a cloud provider?
response: By providing specific logic tailored to the chosen cloud provider, as opposed to relying solely on the hostname or flag-based approach.
title: kubelet

instruction: What are two methods by which container manifests can be provided to kubelet besides through a PodSpec from the apiserver?
response: Manifests can also come via files passed as command line flags and HTTP endpoints checked every 20 seconds.
title: kubelet

instruction: What is the purpose of a PodDisruptionBudget in Kubernetes?
response: PodDisruptionBudget defines the maximum disruption that can be caused to a collection of pods.
title: PodDisruptionBudget

instruction: How does one specify an eviction when using maxUnavailable and minAvailable values within a PodDisruptionBudgetSpec in Kubernetes?
response: An eviction is allowed if at most 'maxUnavailable' pods are unavailable after the eviction, or if at least 'minAvailable' pods will still be available.
title: PodDisruptionBudget

instruction: What does a LabelSelector do within the context of PodDisruptionBudget in Kubernetes?
response: LabelSelector is used to select which pods are subjected to eviction based on their labels, as part of the patch strategy: replace.
title: PodDisruptionBudget

instruction: What does 'kubectl config delete-user' do?
response: Delete the specified user from the kubeconfig.
title: kubectl config delete-user

instruction: How can you specify a namespace scope for the CLI request when using 'kubectl config delete-user'?
response: -n, --namespace string	If present, the namespace scope for this CLI request
title: kubectl config delete-user

instruction: What is the default cache directory used by kubectl and how can it be overridden when using 'kubectl config delete-user'?
response:
'--cache-dir string	Default: $HOME/.kube/cache'
Override with:	kubectl config set cache-dir /path/to/custom/directory
title: kubectl config delete-user

instruction: What is the difference between using a period (.) or slash (/) as separators for sysctl names in Kubernetes v1.23 and later?
response: Starting from Kubernetes version 10, both periods and slashes can be used to separate parts of sysctl names.
title: Using sysctls in a Kubernetes Cluster

instruction: What are some common prefixes found within the /proc/sys/ directory for kernel parameters in a Linux system running on which subsystems?
response: Common prefixes include 'kernel', 'net' (for networking), and 'vm' (for virtual memory).
title: Using sysctls in a Kubernetes Cluster

instruction: How can I get a list of all sysctl parameters available to my Kubernetes cluster nodes that are not acting as control plane hosts, using the kubectl command-line tool?
response: Run `sudo sysctl -a` on each node.
title: Using sysctls in a Kubernetes Cluster

instruction: What does a Variable represent within Kubernetes?
response: A variable used for composition, defined as a named expression.
title: Variable

instruction: Can I use alpha APIs in a stable release like Kubernetes 1.25 for managing devices through the kubelet Device Manager API?
response: No, because they may vanish or change significantly between one minor release and the next.
title: Kubelet Device Manager API Versions

instruction: If I encounter an issue with a device plugin in Kubernetes 1.24 that is not present in v1beta1 of my kubelet Device Manager API versioning, what could be the reason?
response: The server has added additional API calls or removed old ones.
title: Kubelet Device Manager API Versions

instruction: What does upgrading a primary control plane node entail in Kubernetes?
response: Upgrading involves updating the kubelet and Kubectl versions on that specific node.
title: Upgrading kubeadm clusters

instruction: Why is it important to back up components like app-level state stored in databases before performing an upgrade using kubeadm?
response: Backups are a best practice because while upgrading, only Kubernetes internal components get touched and not the workloads.
title: Upgrading kubeadm clusters

instruction: What is required when swapping control plane nodes during a minor version upgrade for any kubelet in terms of draining them?
response: The node (or nodes) being upgraded must first be drained, especially if they are running CoreDNS Pods or other critical workloads.
title: Upgrading kubeadm clusters

instruction: What command can be used to manually edit and save cluster objects in kubeadm?
response: The `kubectl edit` command.
title: Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster

instruction: How do you specify the location of the kubeconfig file for use with kubectl when reconfiguring a Kubeadm cluster using environment variables?
response: Specify it by setting `KUBECONFIG=/etc/kubernetes/admin.conf`.
title: Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster

instruction: What is the potential risk of updating ConfigMaps with unstructured data in kubeadm, and how can this be mitigated?
response: Validation will not be performed upon updates which could lead to typos or indentation mistakes. This risk can be mitigated by carefully following documented API format for component configurations.
title: Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster

instruction: What is the purpose of using 'kubectl certificate' command?
response: To modify certificate resources.
title: kubectl certificate

instruction: Which option allows you to specify multiple groups for impersonation in a kubectl certificate operation?
response: '--as-group strings'
title: kubectl certificate

instruction: What does the '-n' or '--namespace string' flag do when used with the 'kubectl certificate' command?
response: If present, specifies the namespace scope for this CLI request.
title: kubectl certificate

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'Status' object returned by Kubernetes?
response: 'Status' is a return value for calls that don't return other objects.
title: Status

instruction: In which field can you find information about possible extended data associated with reasons in a status response, and how does it function according to the documentation provided?
response: 'details' is where one can find this information. It contains extended data for each reason but its format isn't guaranteed to conform to any schema except that defined by the reason type.
title: Status

instruction: What does an empty 'reason' field indicate in a Kubernetes status response?
response: If the 'reason' value is empty, there is no information available about why this operation failed.
title: Status

instruction: How can I run jobs in parallel using Kubernetes?
response: You can use a Work Queue for coarse parallel processing or an Indexed Job with static work assignment.
title: Run Jobs

instruction: What is the difference between Run Jobs and CronJobs when it comes to running automated tasks in Kubernetes?
response: Run Jobs are used for short-lived, parallel processing jobs while CronJobs run at scheduled intervals.
title: Run Jobs

instruction: How does a Pod failure policy help manage pod failures during job execution on Kubernetes?
response: The Pod failure policy distinguishes between retriable and non-retriable pod failures, allowing for appropriate handling of each case.
title: Run Jobs

instruction: What are preconditions in Kubernetes?
response: Preconditions must be fulfilled before an operation (update, delete, etc.) is carried out.
title: Preconditions

instruction: Which fields specify the target ResourceVersion and UID for a given operation in Kubernetes preconditions?
response: resourceVersion specifies the target ResourceVersion while uid specifies the target UID.
title: Preconditions

instruction: Where can one report an issue with this auto-generated page if it's not functioning as expected in Kubernetes documentation on May 13, 2026?
response: One should open an issue in the GitHub Repository and mention that the page is automatically generated.
title: Preconditions

instruction: How does Kubernetes handle scheduling when a high priority pod cannot be scheduled?
response: The scheduler tries to preempt (evict) lower priority Pods.
title: Pod Priority and Preemption

instruction: What can an administrator do to prevent users from creating pods at the highest possible priorities in untrusted clusters?
response: Use ResourceQuota to limit PriorityClass consumption by default.
title: Pod Priority and Preemption

instruction: How are Pod priority classes used within Kubernetes and what built-in ones exist for critical system components?
response: PriorityClasses map a class name to an integer value of the pod's priority. Built-in PriorityClasses include 'system-cluster-critical' and 'system-node-critical'. These are used to ensure that critical components like master nodes or daemonsets always get scheduled first.
title: Pod Priority and Preemption

instruction: How do you deny a certificate signing request (CSR) in Kubernetes using kubectl?
response: Use the command `kubectl certificate deny <CSR_name>` to deny an existing CSR.
title: kubectl certificate deny

instruction: What options does 'kubectl certificate deny' provide for handling errors in templates when a field or map key is missing?
response: '--allow-missing-template-keys', which defaults to true, allows ignoring any such template errors.
title: kubectl certificate deny

instruction: What does the `--force` option do when used with 'kubectl certificate deny'?
response: '--force' updates the CSR even if it is already denied.
title: kubectl certificate deny

instruction: What is required for Kubernetes Node components to run as a non-root user?
response: Enable Cgroup v2, enable systemd with user session, configure several sysctl values depending on host Linux distribution.
title: Running Kubernetes Node Components as a Non-root User

instruction: How can one check the version of their running Kubernetes server before enabling rootless mode?
response: Enter 'kubectl version' to check the version.
title: Running Kubernetes Node Components as a Non-root User

instruction: Which projects provide functionality required by Kubernetes for unprivileged containers, and what is one of them called specifically designed for running Docker or Podman inside these containers?
response: Sysbox (designed to run Docker/Podman in unprivileged containers without requiring Cgroup v2), sysbox
title: Running Kubernetes Node Components as a Non-root User

instruction: What does a Scale object represent in Kubernetes?
response: A scaling request for a resource.
title: Scale

instruction: In the context of autoscaling, what information is provided by 'replicas' within the ScaleSpec field according to k8s_docs documentation?
response: 'replicas' specifies the desired number of instances for the scaled object.
title: Scale

instruction: How does Kubernetes ensure that a replica count matches in an autoscaling scenario, based on this document snippet from 'k8s_docs'?
response: By using selector which is label query over pods to match the desired number of instances as specified by 'replicas'.
title: Scale

instruction: What is the Container Runtime Interface (CRI) and why does Kubernetes version v1.36 require a CRI-compliant runtime?
response: The Container Runtime Interface (CRI) provides an abstraction layer for container runtimes, ensuring compatibility with different systems that manage containers in clusters like Kubernetes. Starting from Kubernetes 1.36, only runtimes conforming to the CRI are supported.
title: Container Runtimes

instruction: What were dockershim and how has its role changed after being removed as part of Kubernetes v1.24?
response: Dockershim was a component that provided direct integration with Docker Engine within the Kubernetes project, allowing containers to run directly on top of it without using containerd or CRI-based runtimes. It has been removed from Kubernetes as part of version 10 and is no longer supported.
title: Container Runtimes

instruction: What steps are required for enabling IPv4 packet forwarding in the Linux kernel to support cluster networking?
response: To enable IPv4 packet forwarding, you need to set `net.ipv4.ip_forward` sysctl parameter to 1 and apply it system-wide using `sudo sysctl --system`. These settings persist across reboots.
title: Container Runtimes

instruction: What does the `activeDeadlineSeconds` field in a Kubernetes Job specification determine?
response: Specifies the duration in seconds relative to the startTime that the job may be continuously active before the system tries to terminate it.
title: Job

instruction: What is indicated by setting `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` to zero in a Kubernetes Job specification?
response: The grace period for terminating an unresponsive job will be as short as possible, effectively stopping the timer immediately.
title: Job

instruction: How does the success policy affect the cleanup of finished jobs according to the provided documentation on Kubernetes Jobs?
response: The default behavior is that if a successful pod completes its execution and no failures occur, it will be automatically deleted after `completionTimestamp`. However, this can be overridden by setting specific policies in the job specification.
title: Job

instruction: What does an event in Kubernetes generally denote?
response: An event that denotes some state change in the system.
title: Event

instruction: How should consumers treat events and why?
response: Consumers should not rely on timing or continued existence of events with a given reason, as triggers may evolve over time. Events are informative best-effort supplemental data.
title: Event

instruction: What is the deprecatedCount field in an Event object?
response: The deprecatedCount assures backward compatibility with core.v1 Event type.
title: Event

instruction: What is the default behavior of Kubernetes workloads on a Linux node regarding memory swapping?
response: Workloads running as Pods do not and cannot use swap.
title: Linux Node Swap Behaviors

instruction: How can you allow Kubernetes workloads to utilize swap, and what is the default behavior of these workloads with respect to swap usage on a Linux node?
response: To enable swapping for Pods, disable kubelet's default failure upon detecting swap and specify 'LimitedSwap'. By default, Kubernetes workloads do not use swap.
title: Linux Node Swap Behaviors

instruction: What are the implications of setting a Linux node to have LimitedSwap behavior for Kubernetes Pods?
response: Kubernetes workloads can utilize swap memory with an amount determined automatically, but this does not protect them from system-level memory spikes.
title: Linux Node Swap Behaviors

instruction: How can anonymous access to the kubelet HTTPS endpoint be disabled?
response: To disable anonymous access and send '401 Unauthorized' responses to unauthenticated requests, start the kubelet with the --anonymous-auth=false flag.
title: Kubelet authentication/authorization

instruction: What is required for X509 client certificate authentication on the kubelet HTTPS endpoint?
response: To enable X509 client certificate authentication to the kubelet's HTTPS endpoint, start the kubelet with the --client-ca-file flag providing a CA bundle and ensure that apiserver is started with both --kubelet-client-certificate and --kubelet-client-key flags.
title: Kubelet authentication/authorization

instruction: How does Kubelet authorization work after successful authentication?
response: After any request, including an anonymous one, has been successfully authenticated by the kubelet, it is then authorized. The default mode of authorization is 'AlwaysAllow', which permits all requests.
title: Kubelet authentication/authorization

instruction: What is required before starting to configure GMSA for Windows Pods and containers?
response: You need a Kubernetes cluster with configured kubectl command-line tool, expected to have Windows worker nodes.
title: Configure GMSA for Windows Pods and containers

instruction: How are the credentials of Group Managed Service Accounts (GMSA) defined in Kubernetes for use by Pods and containers?
response: Credentials for GMSAs are configured at a cluster-wide scope as Custom Resources using CRD.
title: Configure GMSA for Windows Pods and containers

instruction: What purpose do the webhooks serve when configuring Group Managed Service Accounts (GMSA) in Kubernetes?
response: One mutating webhook expands GMSA references into full credential spec JSON within Pod specifications, and a validating webhook ensures all such references are authorized.
title: Configure GMSA for Windows Pods and containers

instruction: What does the 'client-certificate' field in kubeconfig represent?
response: The path to a client cert file for TLS.
title: kubeconfig (v1)

instruction: Which fields are required when creating clusters and users sections within a Kubernetes configuration (kubeconfig)?
response: Clusters, NamedAuthInfo
title: kubeconfig (v1)

instruction: What is the purpose of 'tokenFile' in kubeconfig?
response: 'TokenFile' is a pointer to a file that contains a bearer token for authentication.
title: kubeconfig (v1)

instruction: What components make up a Kubernetes cluster?
response: A Kubernetes cluster consists of a control plane plus a set of worker machines, called nodes.
title: Cluster Architecture

instruction: Where do the Pods in a Kubernetes cluster run and what hosts them?
response: The worker node(s) host the Pods that are components of the application workload.
title: Cluster Architecture

instruction: What is kube-proxy's role on each node, and under which circumstances might a Kubernetes cluster not require it to run?
response: kube-proxy ensures Service API availability on your cluster network. However, if using third party network plugins that provide their own proxying implementation, the node may not need kube-proxy.
title: Cluster Architecture

instruction: What is the purpose of user namespaces in Kubernetes pods?
response: User namespaces isolate the user running inside a container from the one on the host.
title: User Namespaces

instruction: How can using user namespaces mitigate security vulnerabilities within containers?
response: It reduces damage by ensuring that processes run as root in a container have full privileges only for operations inside the namespace, not affecting other pods or host.
title: User Namespaces

instruction: What Linux kernel version and filesystem types are required to support user namespaces?
response: Linux 6.3 is needed as it supports idmap mounts on used file systems like btrfs, ext4, xfs, fat, tmpfs, overlayfs.
title: User Namespaces

instruction: How can I change the CPU and memory resource requirements of containers within an existing Pod without recreating it?
response: By updating the desired requests and limits in the Pod's specification using kubectl patch, apply, or edit targeting the Pod's resize subresource.
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Containers

instruction: What is represented by a container's 'spec.containers[*].resources' field?
response: 'desired resources for the container'
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Containers

instruction: When changing resource requirements, which Kubernetes status fields reflect the current and allocated values of containers that haven't started or been restarted yet?
response: 'status.containerStatuses[*].resources', 'status.containerStatuses[*].allocatedResources'
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Containers

instruction: How can clients authenticate to the Kubernetes API server according to 'Controlling Access to the Kubernetes API' documentation?
response: Clients must explicitly enable built-in alpha and beta versions of APIs in kube-apiserver configuration for authentication.
title: API Overview

instruction: What does an Alpha version indicate about a Kubernetes API, as described under 'API Changes documentation'?
response: Alpha indicates the software may contain bugs and support might be dropped without notice. It is recommended only for short-lived testing clusters.
title: API Overview

instruction: What is the purpose of abstracting provider-specific code to the cloud-controller-manager binary in Kubernetes?
response: To allow cloud vendors to evolve independently from the core Kubernetes code.
title: Cloud Controller Manager Administration

instruction: Which two components must be set according to the user's usage of external Cloud Controller Manager (CCM)?
response: kubelet and kube-controller-manager
title: Cloud Controller Manager Administration

instruction: What configuration change is required for running cloud-controller-manager?
response: Specify --cloud-provider=external in the cluster setup.
title: Cloud Controller Manager Administration

instruction: How is each worker Pod identified in an Indexed Job for parallel processing?
response: Each worker Pod has a unique pod index number automatically set by the control plane.
title: Indexed Job for Parallel Processing with Static Work Assignment

instruction: Where can I find the completion index of a specific Pod within its container environment variables or files when using downward API in an Indexed Job setup?
response: The annotation batch.kubernetes.io/job-completion-index is available as an environment variable, and it's exposed by default through JOB_COMPLETION_INDEX.
title: Indexed Job for Parallel Processing with Static Work Assignment

instruction: What are the options for a containerized task process to obtain its pod index in an Indexed Job?
response: The container can read either from the environment variable or access it via reading a file containing the completion index, both of which methods rely on downward API.
title: Indexed Job for Parallel Processing with Static Work Assignment

instruction: What does providing DeleteOptions when deleting an API object signify?
response: It indicates that modifications should not be persisted.
title: DeleteOptions

instruction: How is the 'gracePeriodSeconds' field used in Kubernetes and what happens if it's set to zero or omitted for a specific type of resource?
response: The grace period defines how long before deletion an object must exist. If this value is zero, the object will be deleted immediately; otherwise, objects are kept until they reach their specified lifetime.
title: DeleteOptions

instruction: What precautions should one take when using 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' and what does it imply about normal deletion constraints?
response: 'IgnoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' allows for unsafe deletions in case of corrupt objects, ignoring finalizer constraints, skipping precondition checks, and removing the object from storage. It should be used with caution as it may break the cluster if relied upon by workloads expecting normal deletion flow.
title: DeleteOptions

instruction: What are some use cases for deploying an application using a StatefulSet in Kubernetes?
response: Applications requiring stable, unique network identifiers; Stable, persistent storage.
title: StatefulSets

instruction: How does a StatefulSet maintain the sticky identity of its Pods across rescheduling compared to Deployments?
response: StatefulSets assign each Pod with a stable identifier that it retains even if rescheduled, unlike Deployments which do not guarantee this.
title: StatefulSets

instruction: What happens when you delete or scale down a StatefulSet in Kubernetes?
response: The volumes associated with the StatefulSet are not deleted. This is to ensure data safety and persistence across rescheduling of Pods.
title: StatefulSets

instruction: What is the purpose of deploying the Metrics API into a Kubernetes cluster?
response: To provide basic set of CPU and memory metrics for automatic scaling using HPA and/or VPA.
title: Resource metrics pipeline

instruction: How can one view resource usage in a Kubernetes environment without additional tools or APIs?
response: By utilizing the 'kubectl top' command, which provides access to these metrics through the API server.
title: Resource metrics pipeline

instruction: What does Figure 1 illustrate about the architecture of the resource metrics pipeline in Kubernetes?
response: Figure 1 illustrates a diagram where cAdvisor collects container metrics, which are then managed by kubelet and exposed via Metrics API to HPA or VPA for scaling decisions.
title: Resource metrics pipeline

instruction: What is Port Forwarding in Kubernetes and when can it be useful?
response: Port forwarding allows you to connect to a MongoDB server running inside a cluster, which can be beneficial for database debugging.
title: Use Port Forwarding to Access Applications in a Cluster

instruction: Before starting with port forwarding setup on Kubernetes, what prerequisites should one have?
response: You need to have a configured kubectl command-line tool and access to at least two nodes that are not control plane hosts.
title: Use Port Forwarding to Access Applications in a Cluster

instruction: What is the minimum required Kubernetes server version for this tutorial, as per the text?
response: The minimum required Kubernetes server version should be v1.10 or later.
title: Use Port Forwarding to Access Applications in a Cluster

instruction: What does the PodTemplate resource in Kubernetes describe?
response: PodTemplate describes a template for creating copies of a predefined pod.
title: PodTemplate

instruction: In which field of the PodTemplate are metadata and spec defined according to k8s_docs documentation?
response: metadata is in ObjectMeta, while spec is in PodSpec
title: PodTemplate

instruction: What does a PodTemplateList represent as per Kubernetes API conventions mentioned in the document?
response: PodTemplateList represents a list of PodTemplates.
title: PodTemplate

instruction: What is one way an attacker could potentially modify cluster configurations through Kubernetes API Server?
response: An attacker with write access to a named directory or specific URL can introduce new static Pods, which bypass built-in security controls.
title: Kubernetes API Server Bypass Risks

instruction: What are the limitations of Static Pods in terms of accessing other objects within Kubernetes API?
response: Static Pods cannot access other cluster objects. For instance, they can't mount a Secret from the cluster but may perform actions like using hostPath mounts.
title: Kubernetes API Server Bypass Risks

instruction: What happens if a Static Pod fails admission control in Kubernetes?
response: If it fails, the kubelet won't register this pod with the API server. However, the Pod continues to run on its node.
title: Kubernetes API Server Bypass Risks

instruction: How can I set the server field on my_cluster cluster in kubectl config?
response: Use `kubectl config set clusters.my-cluster.server https://1.2.3.4`
title: kubectl config set

instruction: What flag should be used with kubectl to write a given string directly without base64 decoding when setting the client key data?
response: '--set-raw-bytes=true' is required for writing `cert_data_here` as it is.
title: kubectl config set

instruction: What is a PersistentVolume (PV) in Kubernetes?
response: A storage resource provisioned by an administrator, analogous to a node.
title: PersistentVolume

instruction: How can one claim and use a PV for their workload in Kubernetes?
response: By creating a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC), which requests the size of the volume needed. The system then attempts to bind this request with an existing PV.
title: PersistentVolume

instruction: What are some key fields and specifications found within a `PersistentVolume` object in Kubernetes?
response: 'apiVersion': string, 'kind': string (cannot be updated), 'metadata': ObjectMeta', 'spec': PersistentVolumeSpec'
title: PersistentVolume

instruction: What is a PersistentVolumeClaim in Kubernetes?
response: A user's request for and claim to a persistent volume.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim

instruction: How can the status of a PVC be described according to its current information/status?
response: Read-only, represented by PersistentVolumeClaimStatus
title: PersistentVolumeClaim

instruction: What does spec in PersistentVolumeClaimSpec describe about storage devices requested for pods?
response: The common attributes of storage devices and allows a Source for provider-specific attributes.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim

instruction: What are system logs in Kubernetes and why are they useful?
response: System component logs record events happening within the cluster, which can be very helpful for debugging.
title: System Logs

instruction: How does one control log verbosity to see more or less detail in a Kubernetes system?
response: Log verbosity is configurable; however, individual log entries and their formatting may change from release to release as the logging simplification process continues.
title: System Logs

instruction: What happens if you try using deprecated klog command line flags in Kubernetes v1.26?
response: The deprecated `--add-dir-header`, among other flag options, will no longer function as they were removed starting with Kubernetes version 1.26.
title: System Logs

instruction: What does APIVersions list for Kubernetes clients?
response: The versions that are available, allowing clients to discover the API at /api.
title: APIVersions

instruction: How can a client determine which server address is appropriate when multiple matches exist in ServerAddressByClientCIDRs array?
response: In case of multiple matches, clients should use the longest matching CIDR. The server returns only those CIDRs that it thinks that the client can match.
title: APIVersions

instruction: What is the purpose of using ResourceQuota, LimitRange and PersistentVolumeClaim in Kubernetes?
response: To limit how much storage a single namespace can consume.
title: Limit Storage Consumption

instruction: How does adding a 'LimitRange' to a namespace enforce size restrictions on persistent volume claims (PVCs)?
response: It sets minimum and maximum values for requests, rejecting any PVC that exceeds these limits. For example, it would reject a 10Gi request because the max is set at 2Gi.
title: Limit Storage Consumption

instruction: What happens if there's an attempt to create a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) with storage larger than what 'LimitRange' allows?
response: The admission controller that enforces limit ranges will reject this PVC, as it exceeds the set values.
title: Limit Storage Consumption

instruction: What is the primary function of Cloud Controller Manager in Kubernetes?
response: The Cloud Controller Manager embeds cloud-specific control logic and links your cluster into your cloud provider's API.
title: Migrate Replicated Control Plane To Use Cloud Controller Manager

instruction: Why might a migration from kube-controller-manager to cloud-controller-manager be necessary for Kubernetes clusters?
response: All cloud specific controllers must be moved out of the kube-controller-manager as part of the cloud provider extraction effort.
title: Migrate Replicated Control Plane To Use Cloud Controller Manager

instruction: How does Leader Migration facilitate a safe migration between control planes during an upgrade in Kubernetes?

instruction: What is the purpose of client libraries for Kubernetes?
response: To simplify using the Kubernetes REST API without implementing the API calls and request/response types yourself.

instruction: How do most client libraries authenticate to access the Kubernetes cluster's resources?
response: By discovering and using the Service Account or understanding the kubeconfig file format for credentials and API Server address.

instruction: Before starting to assign pods to nodes using Node Affinity in Kubernetes, what prerequisites are required?
response: A configured kubectl command-line tool and a Kubernetes cluster with at least two non-control plane host nodes.

instruction: How can you verify that the chosen node has been labeled correctly for Node Affinity purposes in this tutorial context, specifically labeling it as 'disktype=ssd'?

instruction: What is meant by a Pod having 'requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution' node affinity in Kubernetes?
response: 'RequiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution' means that the scheduler must place this pod on one of these nodes during scheduling, but if no such node is available at runtime (for example due to a failure), it will not be scheduled.

instruction: What is the primary purpose of a ReplicaSet in Kubernetes?
response: To maintain a stable set of replica Pods running at any given time.
title: ReplicaSet

instruction: How does a ReplicaSet identify which new Pods to acquire when needed?
response: By using its selector, and it acquires the first matching pod with no ownerReference or an invalid controller reference in that OwnerReferences field.
title: ReplicaSet

instruction: When should one consider directly manipulating a ReplicaSet instead of relying on Deployments?
response: Only when requiring custom update orchestration or not needing updates at all, as deployments provide more features and are generally recommended.

instruction: What is the primary function of the Kubernetes API server?
response: The Kubernetes API server validates and configures data for api objects including pods, services, replicationcontrollers, etc.

instruction: How does admission control work in Kubernetes?
response: Admission is divided into two phases. In the first phase, only mutating plugins run and in the second phase, only validating plugins run.

instruction: What happens if a provided `--advertise-address` cannot be reached by other cluster members?
response: The API server will not advertise to the rest of the cluster as it must ensure that this address is reachable for proper communication.

instruction: What is the Gateway API and what does it provide?
response: Gateway API is a family of Kubernetes API kinds that offer dynamic infrastructure provisioning, advanced traffic routing, role-oriented configuration mechanism.
title: Gateway API

instruction: How are roles modeled in Gateway API and what responsibilities do they have?
response: Roles in Gateway API are organized around organizational functions. The Infrastructure Provider manages infrastructure for multiple clusters serving tenants, the Cluster Operator handles cluster policies and permissions, while Application Developers manage application-level configurations.
title: Gateway API

instruction: What kind of traffic routing use cases does Gateway API support?
response: Gateway API supports common traffic routing scenarios such as header-based matching and weighted traffic distribution through HTTPRoute and GRPCRoute kinds, which were previously only possible with Ingress using custom annotations.
title: Gateway API

instruction: What is the recommended course of action if I wish to upgrade from Docker Engine via dockershim in Kubernetes v1.24?
response: It's recommended that you either migrate to another runtime or find an alternative means to obtain Docker Engine support.

instruction: When will the version of Kubernetes with dockershim (v1.23) run out of support?

instruction: What is Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) in Kubernetes?

instruction: Which built-0in components are shared responsibility with DRA drivers in Kubernetes?

response: Your cluster should support RBAC as it is necessary for running the tutorial.

instruction: What is a Validating Admission Policy in Kubernetes?

title: Validating Admission Policy

instruction: Which resources are generally made up of a policy according to the documentation on Validating Admission Policy?
response: The three main components making up a policy include: The 'ValidatingAdmissionPolicy' which describes abstract logic, and two parameter resource types that provide specific details for implementation.

instruction: What is the purpose of a ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding in Kubernetes?
response: It links the policy with its parameters together and provides scoping. For instance, it can specify restrictions like requiring an 'owner' label for Pods.

instruction: How can you enable Pod Security Standards checking for a specific namespace in Kubernetes?

kubectl label --overwrite ns example
pod-security

instruction: What is the purpose of a Workload in Kubernetes?
response: A Workload defines what scheduling policies should be applied to groups of Pods, but does not track runtime state itself.

instruction: How are PodGroup objects related to Workloads and what do they represent at runtime?
response: PodGroup objects maintain the runtime scheduling state. Controllers create them from the Workload's PodGroupTemplates, representing groups of pods that should be scheduled together.

instruction: What are some requirements for creating a new workload in Kubernetes?

instruction: What role do controllers play in Kubernetes?

instruction: How does a Job controller bring about change within a Kubernetes cluster?

response: 'Desired state' refers to the desired number of Pods that should be scheduled and run on Nodes, as managed by controllers such as the Job controller.

instruction: What does the 'endpoint' field under TracingConfiguration represent?

instruction: How does a Kubernetes Service ensure loose coupling between dependent Pods and facilitate load balancing?

instruction: What does the 'kubectl drain' command do?
response: 'Drain node in preparation for maintenance. The given node will be marked unschedulable to prevent new pods from arriving.'

instruction: Under what conditions can daemon set-managed pods not be deleted using 'kubectl drain'?
response: 'If there are any pods that are neither mirror pods nor managed by a replication controller, replica set, job, stateful set or daemon set. Then drain will not delete any pods unless you use --force.'
title: kubectl drain

instruction: What is the purpose of 'kubectl uncordon'?
response: 'When you are ready to put the node back into service, use kubectl uncordon, which will make the node schedulable again.'

instruction: What are Kubernetes policies?

instruction: What is the minimum Kubernetes server version required for upgrading Windows nodes?

instruction: How can you prepare a node for maintenance before upgrading it in a cluster with kubeadm installed on Windows?

instruction: What are the steps to upgrade kubelet on a Windows node after draining it?

instruction: What does the PodCertificateRequest API do in Kubernetes?

instruction: How is 'maxExpirationSeconds' defined for a PodCertificateRequest and what are its constraints according to the documentation?
response: 'maxExpirationSeconds' specifies the maximum lifetime permitted for the requested certificate. If not set, it defaults to 86400 seconds (24 hours). The value must be at least 3600 seconds (1 hour) and cannot exceed 7862400 seconds (91 days), with enforcement by kube-apiserver.
title: PodCertificateRequest

instruction: What is the purpose of 'PodCertificateRequestStatus' in a PodCertificateRequest object?
response: 'PodCertificateRequestStatus' contains information about the issued certificate and standard set of conditions, though these specific details are not provided in the given text.

instruction: How can I directly edit any API resource's last-applied configuration annotations using kubectl apply?

instruction: What happens if an error occurs while updating a resource using kubectl apply edit-last-applied?

instruction: What is Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) in Kubernetes and who should have access to DRA related APIs?

title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin

instruction: What should a Kubernetes admin do if they cannot use seamless upgrades for Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) drivers?
response: If the admins can't utilize seamless upgrades, during driver downtime due to updates, pods may not start unless claims were prepared beforehand. Cleanup after last claiming pod also gets delayed until DRA is back.
title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin

instruction: How can I convert a Kubernetes documentation page into the JSON format?
response: You cannot directly convert text from a webpage to structured data like JSON. Instead, you would manually extract relevant information and structure it as per your requirements.
title: Kubeadm Generated

instruction: What is Kubeadm Generated in Kubernetes documentation context?
response: The provided document appears to be related to converting a site into Hugo format using kubeadm, but the specific details about 'Kubeadm Generated' are not given.
title: Kubeadm Generated

instruction: Where can I report problems or suggest improvements for Kubernetes documentation?
response: You should open an issue in the GitHub Repository to report a problem or suggest an improvement regarding Kubernetes documentation.
title: Kubeadm Generated

instruction: What are Kubernetes Mutating Admission Policies?
response: Mutating admission policies offer a declarative, in-process alternative to mutating admission webhooks. They use the Common Expression Language (CEL) to declare resource mutations.
title: Mutating Admission Policy

instruction: What are MutatingAdmissionPolicy and parameter resources?
response: A policy is made up of a general-purpose abstract logic description, called MutatingAdmissionPolicy. Parameter resources provide information for concrete statements in the form 'set label to something like company.example.com'.
title: Mutating Admission Policy

instruction: How are policies scoped within Kubernetes?
response: Policies can be parameterized and scoped using a MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding, which links the policy with its corresponding resources for specificity to certain API kinds or resource types.
title: Mutating Admission Policy

instruction: In Kubernetes v1.36, what new feature is used for DRA status updates?
response: Starting in Kubernetes v1.36, Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) status updates use synthetic subresources.
title: Hardening Guide - Dynamic Resource Allocation

instruction: What are the two types of permissions required to modify 'status.devices' and what verbs do they typically involve?
response: 'resourceclaims/driver', which is verified per-driver, requires node-aware verbs like 'associated-node:update'. For control-plane or multi-node controllers that may update claims from any node, it uses arbitrary verb prefixes such as 'arbitrary-node:patch'.
title: Hardening Guide - Dynamic Resource Allocation

instruction: How does the principle of least privilege apply to DRA drivers and what is an example RBAC pattern for scheduler permissions?
response: The principle of least privilege in DRA enforces that only necessary subresources are accessible, minimizing potential damage from compromised components. An example RBAC pattern granting the appropriate permissions to a Scheduler might look like this: apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1; kind: RoleBinding; metadata: name: scheduler-rolebinding; roleRef: { apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io; kind: ClusterRole; name: system:node-resources } subjects: - kind: ServiceAccount; name: kube-scheduler
title: Hardening Guide - Dynamic Resource Allocation

instruction: What is gang scheduling in Kubernetes and when does it ensure that Pods are scheduled on an 'all-or-nothing' basis?
response: Gang scheduling ensures that a group of Pods are scheduled on an 'all-or-nothing' basis. If the cluster cannot accommodate the entire group or at least the minimum number required, none of the Pods get bound to any node.
title: Gang Scheduling

instruction: What conditions must be met before holding Pods in the PreEnqueue phase according to gang scheduling?
response: Before holding Pods in the PreEnqueue phase for a given group, two conditions need to be satisfied: (1) The referenced PodGroup object should exist and (2) The number of created Pods must meet or exceed 'minCount'.
title: Gang Scheduling

instruction: What happens if enough valid placements are not found during the gang scheduling cycle?
response: If sufficient valid placements for at least the minimum required count ('minCount') of Pods cannot be found, none of these Pods get scheduled. Instead, they're moved to an unschedulable queue and wait for cluster resources to free up.
title: Gang Scheduling

instruction: What does Deployment manage within Kubernetes?
response: A set of Pods to run an application workload, usually one that doesn't maintain state.
title: Deployment

instruction: How can a deployment be paused and resumed in Kubernetes?
response: By setting the 'paused' field to true or false within the DeploymentSpec. When it is set to true, the rollout of new Pods will not start until this value changes.
title: Deployment

instruction: What happens if a deployment fails during scaling up and what can be done?
response: If there are issues with creating additional pod replicas or rolling them out without disruption, you may need to troubleshoot the issue. This could involve checking resource allocation, reviewing error logs in 'kubectl describe' command output for any errors related to scaling.
title: Deployment

instruction: What information does the OwnerReference contain?
response: OwnerReference contains enough information to let you identify an owning object.
title: OwnerReference

instruction: In which scenarios can a dependent be owned by objects in Kubernetes using OwnerReference?
response: An owning object must either belong to the same namespace as the dependent or it should be cluster-scoped, with no requirement for a namespace field.
title: OwnerReference

instruction: What permissions are required when setting the blockOwnerDeletion field of an OwnerReference and what is its default value?
response: To set this field to true, one needs 'delete' permission of the owner. Its default value is false.
title: OwnerReference

instruction: How can credentials be securely distributed in Kubernetes?
response: By using Secrets, which are objects that hold sensitive data like passwords and encryption keys.
title: Distribute Credentials Securely Using Secrets

instruction: What is the recommended tool to convert secret data into a base-64 representation for use with kubectl apply command?
response: A local trusted tool commonly available on most operating systems can be used, but no specific external tools are mentioned in the documentation provided.
title: Distribute Credentials Securely Using Secrets

instruction: What is contained within the 'data' field of a Kubernetes Secret and how does it relate to its usage with pods?
response: 'Data' contains key-value pairs where each value represents encoded sensitive information, such as usernames or passwords. This data can then be injected into Pods securely.
title: Distribute Credentials Securely Using Secrets

instruction: What are taints and how do they affect node affinity in Kubernetes?
response: Taints are properties that attract or repel Pods to a set of nodes, marking the node as unsuitable for pods without matching tolerations.
title: Taints and Tolerations

instruction: How can you add and remove taints from Kubernetes nodes using kubectl?
response: Add a taint with `kubectl taint nodes <node-name> key1=value1:NoSchedule`; to remove, use the same command but change 'NoSchedule' to 'NoSchedule-'. The '-' signifies removal.
title: Taints and Tolerations

instruction: What happens if a manually specified nodeName for a Pod does not have matching tolerations and has NoExecute taint?
response: The kubelet will eject the pod unless there is an appropriate toleration set, as manual specification bypasses the scheduler's consideration of taints.
title: Taints and Tolerations

instruction: What kind of metrics does the resource metrics pipeline provide in Kubernetes?
response: The resource metrics pipeline provides a limited set of metrics related to cluster components such as the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler controller and kubectl top utility.
title: Tools for Monitoring Resources

instruction: How are application performance statistics collected on new clusters in Kubernetes?
response: Application monitoring does not depend on a single solution. On new clusters, you can use resource metrics or full metrics pipelines to collect monitoring statistics.
title: Tools for Monitoring Resources

instruction: What is the role of kubelet in managing pods and containers within Kubernetes?
response: The kubelet acts as a bridge between the Kubernetes master and nodes, managing pods and translating each pod into its constituent containers. It fetches individual container usage statistics from the container runtime through the container runtime interface.
title: Tools for Monitoring Resources

instruction: What should I do if the Kubernetes documentation is not helpful or lacks specific information?
response: Ask your question on Stack Overflow, report a problem in the GitHub Repository by opening an issue.
title: Explore Your App

instruction: How can one provide feedback about usefulness of pages within Kubernetes documentation?
response: Use the 'Was this page helpful?' section to give your thoughts on its utility and effectiveness.

instruction: What was a recent issue reported in relation to the Tutorials landing page, as per the provided text from May 15, 2018?
response: A fix is needed for an unspecified problem with the 'Fix up Tutorials landing page and Tutorials left nav.' (#8557)
title: Explore Your App

instruction: How can you delete StatefulSet pods in Kubernetes?
response: You cannot directly force delete a StatefulSet Pod using kubectl. Instead, the StatefulSet controller is responsible for creating, scaling and deleting members of the StatefulSet.
title: Force Delete StatefulSet Pods

instruction: What are some considerations before manually deleting pods in Kubernetes?
response: Manual deletion should be undertaken with caution as it has potential to violate at most one semantics inherent to a StatefulSet. This can disrupt clustered applications that rely on stable network identity and storage.
title: Force Delete StatefulSet Pods

instruction: What is the recommended way of deleting pods in Kubernetes?
response: The safest method for deletion would be using kubectl delete command with a non-zero value specified for 'pod.Spec.TerminationGracePeriodSeconds'. This ensures that the Pod shuts down gracefully before being deleted.
title: Force Delete StatefulSet Pods

instruction: How can I deploy applications using kubectl?
response: You can use kubectl to inspect and manage cluster resources, as well as view logs.
title: Install Tools

instruction: What is required for installing kind on my computer?
response: Kind requires that you have either Docker or Podman installed.
title: Install Tools

instruction: Where should I report a problem with minikube after installation but before using it to run applications?
response: Report the issue in the GitHub Repository, as suggested for reporting problems and suggesting improvements.
title: Install Tools

instruction: How can you control whether processes within containers gain more privileges than their parent process in Kubernetes?
response: By setting the `allowPrivilegeEscalation` boolean to false.
title: Configure a Security Context for a Pod or Container

instruction: What is a security context and what does it define for Pods or Containers in Kubernetes?
response: A security context defines privilege and access control settings, including Discretionary Access Control (UID/GID permissions), Security Enhanced Linux (SELinux) policies, capabilities given to processes with specific privileges but not all root user privileges, AppArmor program profiles for restricting individual programs' capabilities, Seccomp system call filters, the `allowPrivilegeEscalation` control, and mounting a container’th read-only filesystem.
title: Configure a Security Context for a Pod or Container

instruction: What are some of the security context settings available in Kubernetes?
response: Discretionary Access Control (UID/GID permissions), Security Enhanced Linux policies, capabilities given to processes with specific privileges but not all root user privileges as defined by Linux Capabilities, AppArmor program profiles for restricting individual programs' capabilities, Seccomp system call filters.
title: Configure a Security Context for a Pod or Container

response: A preview of a set of features made available for gathering feedback from the community.

response: No, there are no experimental commands under kubeadm alpha at this time.

instruction: What is the purpose of using 'kubeadm init' in Kubernetes?
response: 'kubeadm init' is used to bootstrap a Kubernetes control-plane node.
title: kubeadm alpha

instruction: How to deploy the Kubernetes Dashboard UI?
response: Deploy it by running `helm repo add kubernetes-dashboard https://kubernetes.github.io/dashboard/` followed by `helm upgrade --install kubernetes-dashboard kubernetes-dashboard/kubernetes-dashboard --create-namespace --namespace kubernetes-dashboard`.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard

instruction: How to access the Kubernetes Dashboard UI?
response: Access it by logging in with a Bearer Token, which can be created following our guide on creating a sample user for this demo. Note that only minimal RBAC configuration is set up by default.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard

instruction: What are the capabilities of Kubernetes Dashboard?
response: It allows deploying containerized applications, troubleshooting them, managing cluster resources, getting an overview of running applications and creating or modifying individual Kubernetes resources like Deployments, Jobs, DaemonSets etc.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard

instruction: What is Pod Overhead in Kubernetes?
response: Pod Overhead refers to the additional system resources consumed by a pod's infrastructure, on top of the container requests and limits.
title: Pod Overhead

instruction: When does Kubernetes set a pod's overhead according to its RuntimeClass?
response: Kubernetes sets a Pod's overhead at admission time based on the associated runtime class's defined 'overhead' field.
title: Pod Overhead

instruction: How is the memory and CPU resource request for pod-test configured in Kata Containers with Firecracker?
response: The workload specifies a RuntimeClass handler of kata-fc, which accounts for an overhead of 120MiB memory and 'cpu' equivalent to 250m.
title: Pod Overhead

instruction: What is node autoscaling in Kubernetes?
response: Node Autoscaling automatically provisions and consolidates Nodes in your cluster to adapt to demand and optimize cost.
title: Node Autoscaling

instruction: How does the Node provisioner decide when to add new nodes to a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The Node provider adds new nodes if there are Pods that can't be scheduled on existing Nodes, especially useful as pod numbers change over time.
title: Node Autoscaling

instruction: What factors do node autoscalers consider when provisioning a new node?
response: Node providers take into account scheduling constraints of Pods and Node-specific configuration imposed by the autoscaler, as well as other triggers like minimum limit configurations.
title: Node Autoscaling

instruction: What does FlowSchema define in Kubernetes?
response: FlowSchema defines the schema of a group of flows.
title: FlowSchema

instruction: How is each flow identified within a FlowSchema according to k8s_docs?
response: Each flow is made up of an API request set with similar attributes and is identified by a pair of strings: the name of the FlowSchema and a 'flow distinguisher'.
title: FlowSchema

instruction: What does `distinguisherMethod` in FlowDistinguisherMethod define within the context of Kubernetes?
response: 'distinguishMethod' defines how to compute the flow distinguisher for requests that match this schema.
title: FlowSchema

instruction: What is the purpose of kube-state-metrics in Kubernetes?
response: kube-state-metrics, an add-on agent to generate and expose cluster-level metrics.
title: Metrics for Kubernetes Object States

instruction: Which metric can provide information about containers running within pods including the container name, image ID, etc. in Kubernetes?
response: kube_pod_container_info
title: Metrics for Kubernetes Object States

instruction: How does one use Prometheus to query for unready pods using kube-state-metrics data?
response: The following PromQL query returns the number of pods that are not ready: count(kube_pod_status_ready{condition="false"}) by (namespace, pod)
title: Metrics for Kubernetes Object States

instruction: What does the 'Topology Affinity Error' indicate in Kubernetes?
response: The error typically occurs when a node has not enough resources available to satisfy the pod's request or due to particular Topology Manager policy constraints.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management

instruction: How can one troubleshoot 'Topology Affinity Error' in Kubernetes?
response: Use kubectl get pods, describe a specific pod using its ID with the command `kubectl describe pod <id>`, or use `kubectl events` to obtain detailed error messages.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management

instruction: What is NUMA and how does it relate to Kubernetes topology management?
response: NUMA stands for Non-Uniform Memory Access, referring to an idea that CPUs might be topologically closer to specific regions of memory due to the physical layout of hardware components. In Kubernetes, managing pod placement policies can help configure hosts so Pods and containers are placed on the correct side of inner boundaries like NUMA domains.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management

instruction: How can you check which nodes are available in your Kubernetes cluster?
response: List the nodes along with their labels using 'kubectl get nodes --show-labels'.
title: Assign Pods to Nodes

instruction: What command would assign a label to one of your existing worker nodes indicating it has an SSD disk type?
response: Use 'kubectl label nodes <your-node-name> disktype=ssd'
title: Assign Pods to Nodes

instruction: How do you create a pod that will be scheduled on the node with the specified SSD storage using YAML configuration file?
response: 'pods/pod-nginx.yaml' contains a Pod definition specifying 'disktype: ssd' in its NodeSelector to ensure it gets placed on an SSD-equipped worker node.
title: Assign Pods to Nodes

instruction: What are the supported network protocols for Kubernetes Services?
response: Kubernetes supports SCTP, TCP (the default), and UDP as network protocols.
title: Protocols for Services

instruction: Which transport protocol is typically used by HTTP/HTTPS LoadBalancer services in special cases with cloud provider support?
response: TCP is the typical transport protocol for these types of Services, but they can also handle traffic at the HTTP level using annotations specific to certain cloud providers.
title: Protocols for Services

instruction: What are some requirements and limitations when configuring multihomed SCTP associations in Kubernetes?
response: Configuring multihomed SCTP associations requires that CNI plugins support multiple interfaces and IP addresses assignment to Pods, as well as special logic for NAT.
title: Protocols for Services

instruction: What is kubectl and what does it interact with in Kubernetes?
response: kubectl is a command-line tool for interacting with Kubernetes clusters, using the Kubernetes API.
title: Introduction to kubectl

instruction: How can you manage workloads declaratively in Kubernetes using kubectl?
response: Use resource configuration files and apply them to modify cluster state reflectively of declared intent for managing resources.
title: Introduction to kubectl

instruction: What is the purpose of draining a node with kubectl, and what does it involve doing in terms of workloads?
response: Draining a node removes all pods from that Node to decommission or debug. It involves moving running Pods elsewhere before making changes.
title: Introduction to kubectl

instruction: What is a Role in Kubernetes?
response: A Role is a namespaced logical grouping of PolicyRules that can be referenced as a unit by a RoleBinding.
title: Role

instruction: How do you create a new role using the API and what parameters are required in the request?
response: To create a new role, send a POST request to /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/roles with 'kind' set as Role and include necessary PolicyRules.
title: Role

instruction: What does the apiVersion field represent in Kubernetes objects?
response: The apiVersion defines the versioned schema of this representation of an object, indicating which versions are recognized by servers for conversion to internal values and potential rejection of unrecognized ones.
title: Role

instruction: How do I apply a network policy using Romana for Kubernetes?
response: To apply network policies, use one of the following methods with Romana.
title: Romana for NetworkPolicy

instruction: What is an example of applying a NetworkPolicy in Romana within a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The page provides examples of how to declare and implement NetworkPolicies using Romana for your Kubernetes deployment.
title: Romana for NetworkPolicy

instruction: Where can I provide feedback or report issues related to the use of Romana with network policies in Kubernetes?
response: You can open an issue in the GitHub Repository if you want to report a problem, suggest improvements, or seek further assistance.
title: Romana for NetworkPolicy

instruction: What is Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) in Kubernetes and what does it allocate?
response: Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) in Kubernetes lets you request and share resources among Pods. These allocated resources are often attached devices like hardware accelerators.
title: Set Up DRA in a Cluster

instruction: What is the minimum required version of Kubernetes for setting up DRA?
response: The minimum required version of Kubernetes to set up Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) is v1.34.
title: Set Up DRA in a Cluster

instruction: Before installing drivers, what should be done regarding device attachment in the cluster when setting up DRA?
response: Directly or indirectly attach devices to your Kubernetes cluster before installing drivers as part of setting up Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA).
title: Set Up DRA in a Cluster

instruction: What is the default port used by Kubernetes components to export traces using OTLP?
response: The IANA OpenTelemetry Port, which is port number 4317.
title: Traces For Kubernetes System Components

instruction: How can you collect and route trace data from a sidecar exporter in Kubernetes without an OpenTelemetry Collector?
response: By specifying the endpoint field with the desired trace backend address directly in the tracing configuration file for components.
title: Traces For Kubernetes System Components

instruction: What can be used to configure authentication details and other headers when sending traces to a back-end without an OpenTelemetry Collector?
response: Environment variables with OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS for trace backend header configuration, and environment variables with OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES for resource attribute configurations.
title: Traces For Kubernetes System Components

instruction: How does Kubernetes v1.26 allow dynamic retrieval of container registry credentials?
response: Kubernetes v1.26 allows the kubelet to dynamically retrieve credentials for a container image registry using exec plugins, which communicate through stdio with versioned APIs.
title: Configure a kubelet image credential provider

instruction: What is required in Kubernetes v1085347 [beta] (v1.34) to send service account token bound to the pod?
response: To enable this feature, the 'KubeletServiceAccountTokenForCredentialProviders' feature gate must be enabled on the kubelet.
title: Configure a kubelet image credential provider

instruction: What information does the `tokenAttributes` field in Kubernetes v1.34 contain regarding service account tokens?
response: The `tokenAttributes` field contains information about the intended audience for the token and whether the plugin requires a pod to have a service account.
title: Configure a kubelet image credential provider

instruction: What is meant by 'container' in Kubernetes?
response: 'Container' refers to an encapsulated software package that includes everything needed to run a specific application, including code, runtime dependencies, libraries, and default settings.
title: Containers

instruction: Why should you not change the running container code according to Kubernetes best practices?
response: Because containers are intended to be stateless and immutable; changes should only occur by building a new image with modifications before recreating the container.
title: Containers

instruction: What is a Container Image in Kubernetes, and what does it include?
response: A 'Container Image' is ready-to0 run software package that contains an application’s code, runtime dependencies, libraries needed for execution, as well as default values for essential settings.
title: Containers

instruction: What is the purpose of IP Masquerade Agent (ip-masq-agent) in Kubernetes?
response: The ip-masq-agent configures iptables rules to hide a pod's IP address behind the cluster node's IP address.
title: IP Masquerade Agent User Guide

instruction: What is NAT and how does it relate to masquerading in Kubernetes?
response: NAT (Network Address Translation) modifies source or destination addresses. Masquerading, a form of NAT used in Kubernetes, hides multiple pod IPs behind one node's address.
title: IP Masquerade Agent User Guide

instruction: What is CIDR notation and how does it apply to the configuration of ip-masq-agent?
response: CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) allows specifying arbitrary-length prefixes in IP addresses. It's used by ip-masq-agent for address representation.
title: IP Masquerade Agent User Guide

instruction: What is ListMeta in Kubernetes?
response: ListMeta describes metadata that synthetic resources must have, including lists and various status objects.
title: ListMeta

instruction: When using the continue feature with a list request, what does it indicate if 'remainingItemCount' is set to 0?
response: If 'remainingItemCount' is set to 0, there are no more remaining items in the list and this field will be left unset.
title: ListMeta

instruction: What should clients not rely on when using the resourceVersion string returned by Kubernetes servers older than v1.15?
response: Clients should not rely on 'remainingItemCount' to estimate the size of a collection, as it may be left unset and is intended for estimation only.
title: ListMeta

instruction: How do kubelets utilize CSIDriver information when mounting volumes in pods?
response: To decide whether pass-through of volume data to the container is necessary.
title: CSIDriver

instruction: Are CSIDriver objects namespaced within Kubernetes clusters, and what API version do they belong to?
response: No, CSIDriver objects are non-namespaced; apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
title: CSIDriver

instruction: What is the primary role of kubelet in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The kubelet acts as the 'node agent' that creates and watches Pods on each node.
title: Kubelet Sync Loop

instruction: How does the Sync Loop function within the context of pod management by kubelet?
response: The sync loop queues work for the Pods assigned to its node, attempting to reconcile their desired state with the actual running containers.
title: Kubelet Sync Loop

instruction: What mechanism does Kubernetes use to run containers and how is this related to kubelet?
response: Kubernetes uses a Container Runtime Interface (CRI) for container execution, wherein kubelet acts as the client instructing the runtime.
title: Kubelet Sync Loop

instruction: How do you create a namespace in Kubernetes?
response: To create a namespace, use the command 'kubectl create namespace <namespace_name>'.
title: Configure Quotas for API Objects

instruction: What is specified within a ResourceQuota object for PersistentVolumeClaims and Services of type LoadBalancer?
response: The configuration file specifies that there can be at most 1 PersistentVolumeClaim, at most 2 services of type LoadBalancer.
title: Configure Quotas for API Objects

instruction: How do you view detailed information about a ResourceQuota in YAML format using kubectl command-line tool?
response: 'kubectl get resourcequota <resource_quota_name> --namespace=<namespace_name> --output=yaml'.
title: Configure Quotas for API Objects

instruction: How can you quickly switch between clusters using the kubectl command?
response: By using the 'kubectl config use-context' command.
title: Configure Access to Multiple Clusters

instruction: What is a configuration file used for in Kubernetes, and what are some other names it might be referred to as?
response: A kubeconfig file is used to configure access to clusters. It can also be called simply 'kubeconfig' or another generic term.
title: Configure Access to Multiple Clusters

instruction: What precautions should you take when using an untrusted kubeconfig file?
response: Inspect the kubeconfig file carefully, much as you would a shell script, to avoid malicious code execution or file exposure.
title: Configure Access to Multiple Clusters

instruction: What HTTP methods are supported by Kubernetes API for primary resources?
response: The Kubernetes API supports GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE.
title: Kubernetes API Concepts

instruction: How does the Kubernetes API notify about changes to a resource efficiently?
response: Kubernetes uses watches for efficient change notifications on resources as a stream of data.
title: Kubernetes API Concepts

instruction: What is the difference between 'resource' and 'collection' in Kubernetes API terminology?
response: 'Resource' refers to a single instance, while 'collection' represents all instances of that resource type.
title: Kubernetes API Concepts

instruction: How can you use kubectl patch to update a node's schedulable status?
response: 'kubectl patch node k8s-node-1 -p '{"spec":{"unschedulable":true}}''
title: kubectl patch

instruction: What is the syntax for updating a container image in a pod using JSON merge patch with positional arrays?
response: 'kubectl patch pod valid-pod --type='json' -p='[{"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/containers/0/image", "value":"new image"}]''
title: kubectl patch

instruction: How can you update the number of replicas for a deployment using kubectl patch?
response: 'kubectl patch deployment nginx-deployment --subresource='scale' --type='merge' -p '{"spec":{{"replicas":2}}}'
title: kubectl patch

instruction: What is the purpose of a Cloud Controller Manager in Kubernetes?
response: The cloud controller manager embeds cloud-specific control logic and links your cluster into your cloud provider's API, separating components that interact with the cloud platform from those only within the cluster.
title: Developing Cloud Controller Manager

instruction: How does abstracting code to a Cloud Controller Manager benefit Kubernetes development?
response: Abstracting provider-specific code allows cloud vendors to evolve independently of the core Kubernetes project, enabling different release paces for features and updates.
title: Developing Cloud Controller Manager

instruction: What is required in `main.go` when developing an out-of-tree Cloud Controller Manager?
response: In `main.go`, you need to create a Go package implementation that satisfies the cloudprovider.Interface, import your customized cloud package, and ensure there's an init block running cloudprovider.RegisterCloudProvider.
title: Developing Cloud Controller Manager

instruction: What does ClusterRole represent in Kubernetes?
response: ClusterRole is a cluster level, logical grouping of PolicyRules that can be referenced as a unit by a RoleBinding or ClusterRoleBinding.
title: ClusterRole

instruction: How are the Rules for a given ClusterRole managed and what happens if changes to these rules occur?
response: If AggregationRule is set, then the Rules are controller-managed. Direct changes to Rules will be stomped by the controller.
title: ClusterRole

instruction: What information does ClusterRoleList provide in Kubernetes documentation for cluster roles?
response: ClusterRoleList is a collection of ClusterRoles, providing an array-like structure to manage multiple instances of ClusterRoles within the system.
title: ClusterRole

instruction: What does a PATCH request body provide for users of Kubernetes?
response: A concrete name and type.
title: Patch

instruction: Where should one report a problem or suggest an improvement for this page according to the feedback section of the document?
response: GitHub Repository, mentioning that the issue may need fixing elsewhere in Kubernetes.
title: Patch

instruction: How can I start a proxy server to access the Kubernetes API?
response: Use this command 'kubectl proxy --port=8080'.
title: Use an HTTP Proxy to Access the Kubernetes API

instruction: What is one way of exploring the Kubernetes API after starting the proxy server using curl?
response: Execute 'curl http://localhost:8080/api/' to get a list of available APIs and versions.
title: Use an HTTP Proxy to Access the Kubernetes API

instruction: How can I retrieve information about pods in my cluster via kubectl command line tool while accessing through an HTTP proxy server on port 8080?
response: 'curl http://localhost:8080/api/v1/namespaces/default/pods' will list all the pods.
title: Use an HTTP Proxy to Access the Kubernetes API

instruction: What is required before beginning to communicate between containers using a shared volume?
response: A Kubernetes cluster with at least two nodes that are not control plane hosts, along with kubectl command-line tool configured for the cluster.
title: Communicate Between Containers in the Same Pod Using a Shared Volume

instruction: What are PriorityClasses in Kubernetes and how do they affect Pod scheduling?
response: PriorityClasses allow you to set the importance of Pods relative to other Pods. If a high priority class is assigned, it increases the chances that its pod will be scheduled over lower priority ones when resources are scarce.
title: Advanced Pod Configuration

instruction: How can one specify priorities for Kubernetes Pods?
response: To assign a PriorityClass to a Pod in Kubernetes, you must define the class with an apiVersion of scheduling.k8s.io/v1 and then set the pod's .spec.priorityClassName field to match this defined priority class.
title: Advanced Pod Configuration

instruction: What is the difference between PriorityClasses and RuntimeClasses in Kubernetes?
response: PriorityClasses determine Pod scheduling importance, while RuntimeClasses specify low-level container runtimes for a Pod. They are used to assign different isolation levels or runtime features.
title: Advanced Pod Configuration
